Names 101-200

101 vṛṣākapiḥ वृषाकपिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He who showers dharma and delight.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

102 ameyātmā अमेयात्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of immeasurable self.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

103 sarvayoga-viniḥsṛtaḥ सर्वयोगविनिःसृतः Yoga, inwardness, and steadiness

Meaning: Beyond all bonds and conditions.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that yoga ripens when attention moves from mental movement to the consciousness that knows it.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by joining breath, steadiness, and remembrance instead of treating yoga as mere technique.

Story: Krishna's teaching on mastery of mind to Arjuna reveals yoga not as escape but as disciplined God-centeredness.

104 vasuḥ वसुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The indwelling wealth.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

105 vasumanāḥ वसुमनाः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He of noble mind.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

106 satyaḥ सत्यः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Truth itself.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a contemplative bridge from sacred word to direct recognition of the self-luminous reality.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by letting the name turn curiosity into discrimination and discrimination into stillness.

Story: The child Dhruva receives mantra and then direct vision, showing that true knowledge matures from remembrance into realization.

107 samātmā समात्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Equal in all.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

108 asaṁmitaḥ असंमितः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Immeasurable.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

109 samaḥ समः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The balanced one.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

110 amoghaḥ अमोघः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Ever effective.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

111 puṇḍarīkākṣaḥ पुण्डरीकाक्षः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Lotus-eyed one.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that sacred radiance is not opposed to truth but can refine the mind toward it.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by seeing beauty as a support for recollection rather than as a distraction to be resented.

Story: The Lord seated with Sri on his chest symbolizes a kingship inseparable from compassion and harmony; this name points to that inseparable splendor.

112 vṛṣakarmā वृषकर्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Performer of righteous acts.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

113 vṛṣākṛtiḥ वृषाकृतिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The form of dharma.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

114 rudraḥ रुद्रः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He who makes beings weep at dissolution.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

115 bahuśirāḥ बहुशिराः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Many-headed.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

116 babhruḥ बभ्रुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He who supports all.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

117 viśvayoniḥ विश्वयोनिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The womb of the universe.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

118 śuciśravāḥ शुचिश्रवाः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of pure fame.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

119 amṛtaḥ अमृतः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Immortal nectar.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

120 śāśvata-sthāṇuḥ शाश्वतस्थाणुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Eternal and firm.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

121 varārohaḥ वरारोहः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of supreme ascent.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

122 mahātapāḥ महातपाः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of great austerity.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

123 sarvagaḥ सर्वगः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Present everywhere.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

124 sarvavid-bhānuḥ सर्वविद्भानुः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: The luminous knower of all.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a contemplative bridge from sacred word to direct recognition of the self-luminous reality.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by letting the name turn curiosity into discrimination and discrimination into stillness.

Story: When Brahma is humbled by Krishna's play in Vraja, he gains clearer understanding of the Lord's inconceivable nature; this name fits that awakening.

125 viṣvaksenaḥ विष्वक्सेनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He whose army is everywhere.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

126 janārdanaḥ जनार्दनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He whom people seek, or who corrects people.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

127 vedaḥ वेदः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: The Veda itself.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a revelation of Vishnu as witness-consciousness and as the light by which scripture and experience become intelligible.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by joining study, self-inquiry, and devotion instead of separating them into different compartments.

Story: Narada repeatedly carries divine insight into confused situations, and his guidance ultimately points back to Vishnu; this name aligns with such illuminating grace.

128 vedavit वेदवित् Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Knower of the Veda.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a revelation of Vishnu as witness-consciousness and as the light by which scripture and experience become intelligible.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by joining study, self-inquiry, and devotion instead of separating them into different compartments.

Story: Akrura's pilgrimage to meet Krishna transforms expectation into revelation; this name fits wisdom that descends as vision, not abstraction alone.

129 avyaṅgaḥ अव्यङ्गः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Without imperfection.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

130 vedāṅgaḥ वेदाङ्गः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Limb of the Veda.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a name that links devotion to viveka, because the Lord is both the revealer of truth and the ground of knowing.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by asking not only what is happening, but who is the knower in whose light the happening is known.

Story: Sanatkumara and the sages seek the supreme truth and find it centered in Narayana; the name suits the Lord who is both teacher and taught-of.

131 vedavit वेदवित् Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Knower of Vedic truth.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a name that links devotion to viveka, because the Lord is both the revealer of truth and the ground of knowing.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by asking not only what is happening, but who is the knower in whose light the happening is known.

Story: In the Gajendra episode, the elephant's past-life memory of prayer returns at the crucial moment; this name reflects divine knowledge awakening dormant wisdom.

132 kaviḥ कविः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: The seer.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a contemplative bridge from sacred word to direct recognition of the self-luminous reality.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by letting the name turn curiosity into discrimination and discrimination into stillness.

Story: Krishna instructs Arjuna on the battlefield, turning despair into discernment; this name belongs to the Lord who teaches inside crisis.

133 lokādhyakṣaḥ लोकाध्यक्षः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Overseer of the worlds.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a contemplative bridge from sacred word to direct recognition of the self-luminous reality.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by letting the name turn curiosity into discrimination and discrimination into stillness.

Story: As Hayagriva, Vishnu restores the Vedas after they are stolen; the story suits names tied to knowledge and sacred speech.

134 surādhyakṣaḥ सुराध्यक्षः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Overseer of the devas.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a contemplative bridge from sacred word to direct recognition of the self-luminous reality.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by letting the name turn curiosity into discrimination and discrimination into stillness.

Story: Kapila teaches Devahuti the way of liberation and right seeing; this name resonates with Vishnu as clarifying intelligence.

135 dharmādhyakṣaḥ धर्माध्यक्षः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Overseer of dharma.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a revelation of Vishnu as witness-consciousness and as the light by which scripture and experience become intelligible.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by joining study, self-inquiry, and devotion instead of separating them into different compartments.

Story: Uddhava receives Krishna's final teachings at the end of the Yadava era; this epithet shines in wisdom given before departure.

136 kṛtākṛtaḥ कृताकृतः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Cause and effect.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

137 caturātmā चतुरात्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Fourfold in manifestation.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

138 caturvyūhaḥ चतुर्व्यूहः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of four emanations.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

139 caturdaṁṣṭraḥ चतुर्दंष्ट्रः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Four-fanged.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

140 caturbhujaḥ चतुर्भुजः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Four-armed.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

141 bhrājiṣṇuḥ भ्राजिष्णुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Effulgent.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

142 bhojanam भोजनम् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The object of enjoyment.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

143 bhoktā भोक्ता Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The enjoyer.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

144 sahiṣṇuḥ सहिष्णुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Forbearing.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

145 jagad-ādijaḥ जगदादिजः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Born before the world.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

146 anaghaḥ अनघः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Sinless.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

147 vijayaḥ विजयः Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Ever victorious.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an affirmation that the ruling Lord is not inert principle but conscious governance present in the moral order.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by asking where divine protection in your life should become ethical steadiness rather than mere emotional comfort.

Story: Krishna lifts Govardhana to shelter Vraja from Indra's storm; this name belongs to divine strength expressed as refuge.

148 jetā जेता Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: The conqueror.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an affirmation that the ruling Lord is not inert principle but conscious governance present in the moral order.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by asking where divine protection in your life should become ethical steadiness rather than mere emotional comfort.

Story: Draupadi's helpless cry draws Krishna's intervention in the assembly; the name suits the Lord who protects dignity when human power fails.

149 viśvayoniḥ विश्वयोनिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Source of the universe.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

150 punarvasuḥ पुनर्वसुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He who dwells again and again in beings.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

151 upendraḥ उपेन्द्रः Vamana and Trivikrama names

Meaning: Younger brother of Indra.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a form-centered praise that steadies devotion while quietly training the mind toward the limitless reality behind form.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using personal devotion as the emotional purification that prepares the mind for knowledge.

Story: Vamana arrives as a small brahmacharin before Bali, showing how divine vastness can choose apparent littleness.

152 vāmanaḥ वामनः Vamana and Trivikrama names

Meaning: The dwarf incarnation.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a form-centered praise that steadies devotion while quietly training the mind toward the limitless reality behind form.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using personal devotion as the emotional purification that prepares the mind for knowledge.

Story: The three strides that reclaim the worlds make pervasion, measure, and sovereignty visible at once.

153 prāṁśuḥ प्रांशुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Tall and vast.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

154 amoghaḥ अमोघः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Whose acts are never futile.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

155 śuciḥ शुचिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Pure.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

156 ūrjitaḥ ऊर्जितः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Mighty and full of vigor.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

157 atīndraḥ अतीन्द्रः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Beyond Indra.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

158 saṅgrahaḥ सङ्ग्रहः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The gatherer, integrator.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

159 sargaḥ सर्गः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Creation.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

160 dhṛtātmā धृतात्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Self-sustained and steadfast.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

161 niyamaḥ नियमः Time, rhythm, and ordinance

Meaning: Order and discipline.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to seeing that calendar, causation, and destiny belong to vyavahara, not to the absolute as such.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by remembering that what changes in time is not the deepest self.

Story: Rama's exile ripens many destinies through disciplined endurance; this name suits a Lord whose order unfolds through season and patience.

162 yamaḥ यमः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The controller.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

163 vedyaḥ वेद्यः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He who is to be known.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

164 vaidyaḥ वैद्यः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The healer.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

165 sadāyogī सदायोगी Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Forever united.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

166 vīrahā वीरहा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Slayer of the mighty.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

167 mādhavaḥ माधवः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Lord of sweet wisdom and springlike grace.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a name that links devotion to viveka, because the Lord is both the revealer of truth and the ground of knowing.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by asking not only what is happening, but who is the knower in whose light the happening is known.

Story: The child Dhruva receives mantra and then direct vision, showing that true knowledge matures from remembrance into realization.

168 madhuḥ मधुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Sweetness itself.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

169 atīndriyaḥ अतीन्द्रियः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Beyond the senses.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

170 mahāmāyaḥ महामायः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Master of wondrous power.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

171 mahotsāhaḥ महोत्साहः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of great enthusiasm.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

172 mahābalaḥ महाबलः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of immense strength.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

173 mahābuddhiḥ महाबुद्धिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of vast intelligence.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

174 mahāvīryaḥ महावीर्यः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of supreme heroism.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

175 mahāśaktiḥ महाशक्तिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of immense power.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

176 mahādyutiḥ महाद्युतिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of great radiance.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

177 anirdeśya-vapuḥ अनिर्देश्यवपुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of indescribable form.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

178 śrīmān श्रीमान् Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Ever endowed with Sri.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a name in which Sri becomes a contemplative symbol of harmony, grace, and all-sided completeness.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by redefining prosperity as inward clarity plus wise stewardship of what has been given.

Story: Tulasi's devotion becomes eternally joined to Vishnu's worship, showing that auspiciousness includes purity of offering and love.

179 ameyātmā अमेयात्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of immeasurable essence.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

180 mahādridhṛk महाद्रिधृक् Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Bearer of the great mountain.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a way of reading the elements as nama-rupa dependent on one underlying reality.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by turning sky, light, wind, and water into meditation prompts instead of background scenery.

Story: Krishna dances upon Kaliya in the Yamuna, purifying poisoned waters; this name aligns with divine mastery within nature.

181 maheṣvāsaḥ महेष्वासः Weapons, emblems, and corrective power

Meaning: Wielder of the great bow.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a symbolic praise of the Lord's power to cut avidya, protect dharma, and remove inner obstruction.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by letting discrimination cut confusion before it grows into habit.

Story: Krishna's discus severs Shishupala's arrogance at the Rajasuya after long patience, making this name one of measured justice.

182 mahībhartā महीभर्ता Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Supporter of the earth.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a way of reading the elements as nama-rupa dependent on one underlying reality.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by turning sky, light, wind, and water into meditation prompts instead of background scenery.

Story: Govardhana itself becomes a sign of worship when Krishna turns the community toward the mountain, cows, and rain that sustain life.

183 śrīnivāsaḥ श्रीनिवासः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Abode of Sri.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that sacred radiance is not opposed to truth but can refine the mind toward it.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by seeing beauty as a support for recollection rather than as a distraction to be resented.

Story: The Samudra Manthana produces many treasures, yet Lakshmi's choosing of Vishnu reveals where true prosperity ultimately rests.

184 satāṁ gatiḥ सतां गतिः Freedom, destination, and moksha

Meaning: Goal of the noble.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that the Lord praised here is also the final truth in whose knowledge bondage ends.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using sorrow itself as a doorway to discern the difference between passing states and the real.

Story: Ajamila's last-minute remembrance opens a path beyond the mechanics of punishment; this name points to grace beyond despair.

185 aniruddhaḥ अनिरुद्धः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Unobstructed.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

186 surānandaḥ सुरानन्दः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Delight of the devas.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

187 govindaḥ गोविन्दः Krishna-centered names

Meaning: Protector of cows, earth, and senses.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a form-centered praise that steadies devotion while quietly training the mind toward the limitless reality behind form.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using personal devotion as the emotional purification that prepares the mind for knowledge.

Story: Yashoda binds the child with rope in the Damodara episode, revealing a Lord conquered only by love.

188 govidāṁ patiḥ गोविदां पतिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Lord of those who know.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

189 marīciḥ मरीचिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Ray of light.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

190 damanaḥ दमनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The subduer.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

191 haṁsaḥ हंसः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The swan, the pure discerner.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

192 suparṇaḥ सुपर्णः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The beautiful-winged one.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

193 bhujagottamaḥ भुजगोत्तमः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Best among serpents.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that cosmic texture is not independent substance but appearance upheld in consciousness.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by observing change in the world while remembering the changeless that makes change knowable.

Story: The descent of the Ganga through divine mediation reflects a cosmos where sacred waters carry grace into the world.

194 hiraṇyanābhaḥ हिरण्यनाभः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Golden-naveled.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

195 sutapāḥ सुतपाः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of excellent austerity.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

196 padmanābhaḥ पद्मनाभः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Lotus-naveled.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

197 prajāpatiḥ प्रजापतिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Lord of creatures.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

198 amṛtyuḥ अमृत्युः Freedom, destination, and moksha

Meaning: Deathless.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a direct pointer toward moksha as knowledge of reality rather than as a mere improved condition inside samsara.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by asking what you still take to be absolutely necessary even though it cannot survive change.

Story: The Four Kumaras attain devotion through the fragrance of Tulasi at Vishnu's feet, showing liberation softened into loving nearness.

199 sarvadṛk सर्वदृक् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The all-seeing one.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

200 siṁhaḥ सिंहः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The lion.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

203 sthiraḥ स्थिरः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Steady.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

204 ajaḥ अजः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Unborn.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

205 durmarṣaṇaḥ दुर्मर्षणः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Intolerant of adharma.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

206 śāstā शास्ता Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: The teacher and ruler.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a contemplative bridge from sacred word to direct recognition of the self-luminous reality.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by letting the name turn curiosity into discrimination and discrimination into stillness.

Story: When Brahma is humbled by Krishna's play in Vraja, he gains clearer understanding of the Lord's inconceivable nature; this name fits that awakening.

207 viśrutātmā विश्रुतात्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Whose glory is well known.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

208 surārihā सुरारिहा Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Destroyer of the enemies of the devas.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a saguna-Ishvara name through which grace, order, and dharmic correction become meditatively approachable.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by trusting order without becoming passive, and doing your dharma while leaving total control to the Lord.

Story: Gajendra's appeal from the lake brings Vishnu immediately on Garuda; this epithet lives in that swift rescue of the desperate.

209 guruḥ गुरुः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: The great teacher.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a revelation of Vishnu as witness-consciousness and as the light by which scripture and experience become intelligible.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by joining study, self-inquiry, and devotion instead of separating them into different compartments.

Story: Narada repeatedly carries divine insight into confused situations, and his guidance ultimately points back to Vishnu; this name aligns with such illuminating grace.

210 gurutamaḥ गुरुतमः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: The greatest among teachers.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a name that links devotion to viveka, because the Lord is both the revealer of truth and the ground of knowing.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by asking not only what is happening, but who is the knower in whose light the happening is known.

Story: Akrura's pilgrimage to meet Krishna transforms expectation into revelation; this name fits wisdom that descends as vision, not abstraction alone.

211 dhāma धाम Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The radiant abode.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

212 satyaḥ सत्यः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Truth.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a name that links devotion to viveka, because the Lord is both the revealer of truth and the ground of knowing.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by asking not only what is happening, but who is the knower in whose light the happening is known.

Story: Sanatkumara and the sages seek the supreme truth and find it centered in Narayana; the name suits the Lord who is both teacher and taught-of.

213 satyaparākramaḥ सत्यपराक्रमः Rama-centered names

Meaning: Whose valor is true and unfailing.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a form-centered praise that steadies devotion while quietly training the mind toward the limitless reality behind form.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using personal devotion as the emotional purification that prepares the mind for knowledge.

Story: Rama's protection of the sages in Dandaka reveals divine kingship expressed through service.

214 nimiṣaḥ निमिषः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The wink or closing of the eyes.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

215 animiṣaḥ अनिमिषः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Ever wakeful, unwinking.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

216 sragvī स्रग्वी Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Wearer of garlands.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

217 vācaspatir-udāradhīḥ वाचस्पतिरुदारधीः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Lord of speech and expansive intelligence.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a revelation of Vishnu as witness-consciousness and as the light by which scripture and experience become intelligible.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by joining study, self-inquiry, and devotion instead of separating them into different compartments.

Story: In the Gajendra episode, the elephant's past-life memory of prayer returns at the crucial moment; this name reflects divine knowledge awakening dormant wisdom.

218 agraṇīḥ अग्रणीः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The foremost guide.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

219 grāmaṇīḥ ग्रामणीः Rama-centered names

Meaning: Leader of groups and beings.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that avatara names are not metaphysical limitations of Brahman but compassionate manifestations for seekers.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by allowing the story to hold the heart steady until deeper understanding becomes possible.

Story: The defeat of Ravana manifests justice that answers long-standing misuse of power.

220 śrīmān श्रीमान् Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Ever glorious with Sri.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that sacred radiance is not opposed to truth but can refine the mind toward it.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by seeing beauty as a support for recollection rather than as a distraction to be resented.

Story: In the story of Andal's longing for Ranganatha, divine beauty becomes an object of total surrender; this name resonates with that magnetic grace.

221 nyāyaḥ न्यायः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Justice and right method.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

222 netā नेता Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The leader.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

223 samīraṇaḥ समीरणः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: The life-giving wind.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a contemplative hint that what seems manifold in nature rests in one unfragmented truth.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by allowing elemental experience to point beyond itself toward the one reality it depends on.

Story: The serpent Ananta becomes Vishnu's couch, revealing even immense natural force as restful service in the divine presence.

224 sahasramūrdhā सहस्रमूर्धा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Thousand-headed.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

225 viśvātmā विश्वात्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Soul of the universe.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

226 sahasrākṣaḥ सहस्राक्षः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Thousand-eyed.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

227 sahasrapāt सहस्रपात् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Thousand-footed.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

228 āvartanaḥ आवर्तनः Time, rhythm, and ordinance

Meaning: He who turns the wheel of existence.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that the Lord presides over temporal process while remaining untouched by its mutations.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name when restless about results; it helps distinguish eternal value from temporary sequence.

Story: Vamana's measured three steps transform cosmic space into sacred order; this epithet reflects the Lord as measurer and ordainer.

229 nivṛttātmā निवृत्तात्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Withdrawn from worldly involvement.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

230 saṁvṛtaḥ संवृतः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Veiled by his own maya.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

231 sampramardanaḥ सम्प्रमर्दनः Rama-centered names

Meaning: Crusher of enemies.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a form-centered praise that steadies devotion while quietly training the mind toward the limitless reality behind form.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using personal devotion as the emotional purification that prepares the mind for knowledge.

Story: Sabari's darshana shows Rama's tenderness toward humble devotion.

232 ahaḥ-saṁvartakaḥ अहःसंवर्तकः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He who brings forth the day.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

233 vahniḥ वह्निः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Fire.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a contemplative hint that what seems manifold in nature rests in one unfragmented truth.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by allowing elemental experience to point beyond itself toward the one reality it depends on.

Story: Garuda's service shows sky, speed, and bird-power offered wholly to the Lord; this name resonates with that elevation.

234 anilaḥ अनिलः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Wind.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a way of reading the elements as nama-rupa dependent on one underlying reality.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by turning sky, light, wind, and water into meditation prompts instead of background scenery.

Story: The moonlike cooling of Krishna's presence in Vraja often follows scenes of heat and turmoil; such names fit the soothing aspect of divinity in nature.

235 dharaṇīdharaḥ धरणीधरः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Supporter of the earth.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a way of reading the elements as nama-rupa dependent on one underlying reality.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by turning sky, light, wind, and water into meditation prompts instead of background scenery.

Story: In the forest episodes of the Ramayana, trees, rivers, and mountains become witnesses to Rama's dharma; this name turns the landscape into theology.

236 suprasādaḥ सुप्रसादः Compassion, refuge, and tenderness

Meaning: Easily pleased and gracious.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a way of praising the Lord whose grace purifies the mind and makes brahma-jnana possible.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by accepting grace as a real part of sadhana and allowing humility to soften self-judgment.

Story: Sudama's simple offering of flattened rice is received by Krishna with overwhelming tenderness; this name fits the Lord who values love over polish.

237 prasannātmā प्रसन्नात्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of serene self.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

238 viśvadhṛk विश्वधृक् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Supporter of the universe.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

239 viśvabhuk विश्वभुक् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Enjoyer or sustainer of the universe.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

240 vibhuḥ विभुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The all-pervading mighty one.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

241 satkartā सत्कर्ता Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Honorer of the good.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

242 satkṛtaḥ सत्कृतः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He who is rightly worshipped.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

243 sādhuḥ साधुः Compassion, refuge, and tenderness

Meaning: The righteous and gentle one.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a way of praising the Lord whose grace purifies the mind and makes brahma-jnana possible.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by accepting grace as a real part of sadhana and allowing humility to soften self-judgment.

Story: Sabari's patient devotion is fulfilled when Rama accepts her offering without contempt; the name reflects divine gentleness toward sincerity.

244 jahnuḥ जह्नुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He who withdraws the universe into himself.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Meaning: Refuge of all beings.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

250 śiṣṭakṛt शिष्टकृत् Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Protector and maker of the noble.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a support for devotion to the Lord who removes obstacles to knowledge by preserving righteousness.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by seeing the removal of confusion as one of the Lord's most important forms of protection.

Story: Rama defends the sages of the forest and keeps his father's word despite hardship; this name honors steadfast protective dharma.

251 śuciḥ शुचिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Pure.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.
