How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

422 saṁvatsaraḥ संवत्सरः Time, rhythm, and ordinance

Meaning: The year, cyclical time.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to seeing that calendar, causation, and destiny belong to vyavahara, not to the absolute as such.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by remembering that what changes in time is not the deepest self.

Story: The cyclic creation and dissolution described in the Puranas show kalpas moving within Narayana's will; this name belongs to the Lord behind vast rhythms.

423 dakṣaḥ दक्षः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Skillful and efficient.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

424 viśrāmaḥ विश्रामः Rama-centered names

Meaning: Rest and repose.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an upasana-rich name: form is honored as a valid doorway through which the mind approaches the formless truth.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by meditating on the form lovingly while remembering that the form is a doorway, not a boundary.

Story: The defeat of Ravana manifests justice that answers long-standing misuse of power.

425 viśvadakṣiṇaḥ विश्वदक्षिणः Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: The all-offering Lord to whom all gifts go.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a way of seeing sacrifice, offerer, offering, and fruit as resting in one Brahman through Ishvara.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by offering speech, labor, food, and restraint inwardly to the Lord rather than performing them with vanity.

Story: When Krishna teaches that offerings made with devotion are accepted even if simple, this epithet becomes inward and universal.

426 vistāraḥ विस्तारः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Expansion, vast spread.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

427 sthāvarasthāṇuḥ स्थावरस्थाणुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Stable amidst all that moves and stands.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

428 pramāṇam प्रमाणम् Rama-centered names

Meaning: The standard and proof.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that avatara names are not metaphysical limitations of Brahman but compassionate manifestations for seekers.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by allowing the story to hold the heart steady until deeper understanding becomes possible.

Story: Sabari's darshana shows Rama's tenderness toward humble devotion.

429 bījam-avyayam बीजमव्ययम् Freedom, destination, and moksha

Meaning: The imperishable seed.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a contemplative invitation to move from devotional dependence to liberating recognition without discarding devotion.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by remembering that devotion need not stop at consolation; it can culminate in liberating knowledge.

Story: Uddhava is guided beyond grief by Krishna's final teachings; the name suits liberation through understanding and surrender.

430 arthaḥ अर्थः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: True meaning and value.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

431 anarthaḥ अनर्थः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Beyond dependence on worldly gain.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

432 mahākośaḥ महाकोशः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Great treasury.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

433 mahābhogaḥ महाभोगः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Great enjoyer and source of delight.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

434 mahādhanaḥ महाधनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Great wealth.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

435 anirviṇṇaḥ अनिर्विण्णः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Never despondent.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

436 sthaviṣṭhaḥ स्थविष्ठः Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: The most massive and firm.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that ritual is fulfilled when it matures into inner offering and knowledge of the divine center.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by treating daily action as worship that should purify the mind, not merely accumulate results.

Story: Bali's gift to Vamana turns a ritual act into total surrender; this name fits the Lord who receives both offering and offerer.

437 bhūḥ भूः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: The earth; pure being.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that cosmic texture is not independent substance but appearance upheld in consciousness.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by observing change in the world while remembering the changeless that makes change knowable.

Story: Kurma supports Mount Mandara during the churning, turning mountain and ocean alike into instruments of divine purpose.

438 dharmayūpaḥ धर्मयूपः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The sacrificial post of righteousness.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

439 mahāmakhaḥ महामखः Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: The great sacrifice.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an Upasana-friendly name that pulls outward worship back toward the one inner receiver of all sacred action.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by remembering that the deepest oblation is ego-centeredness itself.

Story: The sages at Naimisharanya keep sacrificial time alive through remembrance of Narayana; the name belongs to the divine heart of ritual continuity.

440 nakṣatranemiḥ नक्षत्रनेमिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Axis of the stars.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

441 nakṣatrī नक्षत्री Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Lord of the constellations.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

442 kṣamaḥ क्षमः Compassion, refuge, and tenderness

Meaning: Patient and forgiving.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a way of praising the Lord whose grace purifies the mind and makes brahma-jnana possible.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by accepting grace as a real part of sadhana and allowing humility to soften self-judgment.

Story: Vidura receives Krishna with love unmeasured by status, and Krishna accepts him completely; this name belongs to that nearness.

443 kṣāmaḥ क्षामः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Lean through austerity, or subtle.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

444 samīhanaḥ समीहनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He of deliberate striving.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

445 yajñaḥ यज्ञः Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: Sacrifice itself.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that ritual is fulfilled when it matures into inner offering and knowledge of the divine center.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by treating daily action as worship that should purify the mind, not merely accumulate results.

Story: In Ambarisha's observance of Ekadashi and Dvadashi, discipline itself becomes worship; this name suits Vishnu as the fulfiller of vowed offering.

446 ijyaḥ इज्यः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He who is worthy of worship.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

447 mahejyaḥ महेज्यः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Most worthy of worship.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

448 kratuḥ क्रतुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Sacred rite and resolve.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

449 satram सत्रम् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The great sacrificial session.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

450 satāṁ gatiḥ सतां गतिः Freedom, destination, and moksha

Meaning: Goal of the good.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a direct pointer toward moksha as knowledge of reality rather than as a mere improved condition inside samsara.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by asking what you still take to be absolutely necessary even though it cannot survive change.

Story: Bharata's later detachment after the deer episode illustrates how the Lord patiently leads the soul beyond subtle attachments; this name resonates there.

451 sarvadarśī सर्वदर्शी Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: All-seeing.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

452 vimuktātmā विमुक्तात्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Whose self is ever free.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

453 sarvajñaḥ सर्वज्ञः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Omniscient.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

454 jñānam-uttamam ज्ञानमुत्तमम् Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Supreme knowledge.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a name that links devotion to viveka, because the Lord is both the revealer of truth and the ground of knowing.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by asking not only what is happening, but who is the knower in whose light the happening is known.

Story: Uddhava receives Krishna's final teachings at the end of the Yadava era; this epithet shines in wisdom given before departure.

455 suvrataḥ सुव्रतः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of excellent vows.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

456 sumukhaḥ सुमुखः Compassion, refuge, and tenderness

Meaning: Of gracious face.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a tender name through which the limitless becomes spiritually intimate without ceasing to be limitless.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by letting affection for Vishnu make the mind subtle enough for contemplation.

Story: Draupadi's appeal is answered not because her situation is elegant but because her surrender is total; this name reveals compassionate response.

457 sūkṣmaḥ सूक्ष्मः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Subtle.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

458 sughoṣaḥ सुघोषः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of beautiful sound.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

459 sukhadaḥ सुखदः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Giver of happiness.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

460 suhṛt सुहृत् Compassion, refuge, and tenderness

Meaning: True friend.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a way of praising the Lord whose grace purifies the mind and makes brahma-jnana possible.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by accepting grace as a real part of sadhana and allowing humility to soften self-judgment.

Story: Gajendra is heard across species and circumstance; the story matches a name that refuses to limit divine care.

461 manoharaḥ मनोहरः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Captivating to the mind.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

462 jitakrodhaḥ जितक्रोधः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Master of anger.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

463 vīrabāhuḥ वीरबाहुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Strong-armed hero.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

464 vidāraṇaḥ विदारणः Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: The cleaver, destroyer of evil.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an affirmation that the ruling Lord is not inert principle but conscious governance present in the moral order.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by asking where divine protection in your life should become ethical steadiness rather than mere emotional comfort.

Story: Krishna lifts Govardhana to shelter Vraja from Indra's storm; this name belongs to divine strength expressed as refuge.

465 svāpanaḥ स्वापनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He who causes rest and sleep.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

466 svavaśaḥ स्ववशः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Under his own control alone.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

467 vyāpī व्यापी Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: All-pervasive.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

468 naikātmā नैकात्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Manifold in self-expression.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

469 naikakarmakṛt नैककर्मकृत् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Performer of many acts.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

470 vatsaraḥ वत्सरः Time, rhythm, and ordinance

Meaning: The year, the cycle.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a name that places time, sequence, and change inside the governance of timeless reality.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by accepting timing, patience, and ripening as part of a sacred process rather than an insult to your plans.

Story: Krishna delays open confrontation with Kamsa until the right moment, showing time used as instrument rather than obstacle.

471 vatsalaḥ वत्सलः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Tenderly affectionate.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

472 vatsī वत्सी Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He who cherishes or owns the calf-like devotees.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

473 ratnagarbhaḥ रत्नगर्भः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Womb of jewels.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

474 dhaneśvaraḥ धनेश्वरः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Lord of wealth.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

475 dharmagup धर्मगुप् Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Protector of dharma.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a support for devotion to the Lord who removes obstacles to knowledge by preserving righteousness.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by seeing the removal of confusion as one of the Lord's most important forms of protection.

Story: Draupadi's helpless cry draws Krishna's intervention in the assembly; the name suits the Lord who protects dignity when human power fails.

476 dharmakṛt धर्मकृत् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Maker of dharma.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

477 dharmī धर्मी Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Embodiment of dharma.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

478 sat सत् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Being, the real.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

479 asat असत् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The unmanifest beyond apparent being.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

480 kṣaram क्षरम् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The perishable field.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

481 akṣaram अक्षरम् Freedom, destination, and moksha

Meaning: The imperishable reality.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that the Lord praised here is also the final truth in whose knowledge bondage ends.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using sorrow itself as a doorway to discern the difference between passing states and the real.

Story: In Vaikuntha descriptions, liberated beings serve without rivalry or lack; this name points to that fulfilled state.

482 avijñātā अविज्ञाता Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Not fully known by ordinary understanding.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

483 sahasrāṁśuḥ सहस्रांशुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Thousand-rayed.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

484 vidhātā विधाता Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The ordainer.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

485 kṛtalakṣaṇaḥ कृतलक्षणः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Marked by all auspicious signs.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a name in which Sri becomes a contemplative symbol of harmony, grace, and all-sided completeness.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by redefining prosperity as inward clarity plus wise stewardship of what has been given.

Story: At the emergence of Lakshmi from the milk ocean, she chooses Vishnu as her eternal abode; this name naturally carries the glow of that union.

486 gabhastinemiḥ गभस्तिनेमिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Hub of the rays of light.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

487 sattvasthaḥ सत्त्वस्थः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Established in purity and clarity.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

488 siṁhaḥ सिंहः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The lion.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

489 bhūtamaheśvaraḥ भूतमहेश्वरः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Great lord of all beings.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

490 ādidevaḥ आदिदेवः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The primordial deity.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

491 mahādevaḥ महादेवः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The great shining lord.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

492 deveśaḥ देवेशः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Lord of the devas.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

493 devabhṛd-guruḥ देवभृद्गुरुः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Teacher even of the gods who support heaven.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a contemplative bridge from sacred word to direct recognition of the self-luminous reality.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by letting the name turn curiosity into discrimination and discrimination into stillness.

Story: The child Dhruva receives mantra and then direct vision, showing that true knowledge matures from remembrance into realization.

494 uttaraḥ उत्तरः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The one who carries across, the higher one.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

495 gopatiḥ गोपतिः Krishna-centered names

Meaning: Lord of cows, earth, and beings.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an upasana-rich name: form is honored as a valid doorway through which the mind approaches the formless truth.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by meditating on the form lovingly while remembering that the form is a doorway, not a boundary.

Story: Krishna's flute summons the hearts of Vraja, showing beauty that governs without coercion.

496 goptā गोप्ता Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Protector.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a saguna-Ishvara name through which grace, order, and dharmic correction become meditatively approachable.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by trusting order without becoming passive, and doing your dharma while leaving total control to the Lord.

Story: Gajendra's appeal from the lake brings Vishnu immediately on Garuda; this epithet lives in that swift rescue of the desperate.

497 jñānagamyaḥ ज्ञानगम्यः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Attainable through knowledge.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a revelation of Vishnu as witness-consciousness and as the light by which scripture and experience become intelligible.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by joining study, self-inquiry, and devotion instead of separating them into different compartments.

Story: When Brahma is humbled by Krishna's play in Vraja, he gains clearer understanding of the Lord's inconceivable nature; this name fits that awakening.

498 purātanaḥ पुरातनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Ancient and primordial.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

499 śarīrabhūtabhṛt शरीरभूतभृत् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Sustainer of embodied beings.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

500 bhoktā भोक्ता Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The enjoyer.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

Names 501-600

501 kapīndraḥ कपीन्द्रः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Lord among beings of strength; also monkey-lord ally.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

502 bhūridakṣiṇaḥ भूरिदक्षिणः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Bountiful giver.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

503 somapaḥ सोमपः Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: Drinker of soma.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an Upasana-friendly name that pulls outward worship back toward the one inner receiver of all sacred action.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by remembering that the deepest oblation is ego-centeredness itself.

Story: In the churning of the ocean, gods and asuras labor for nectar, but the whole enterprise reaches order only through Vishnu's oversight; this name shows the Lord as the hidden receiver of sacrifice.

504 amṛtapaḥ अमृतपः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Drinker of immortality.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

505 somaḥ सोमः Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: The moonlike, soothing one.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a way of seeing sacrifice, offerer, offering, and fruit as resting in one Brahman through Ishvara.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by offering speech, labor, food, and restraint inwardly to the Lord rather than performing them with vanity.

Story: As Yajna in primordial times, Vishnu upholds cosmic order through sacrificial harmony; the name fits the deity who is both rite and fruit.

506 purujit पुरुजित् Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Conqueror of many.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a saguna-Ishvara name through which grace, order, and dharmic correction become meditatively approachable.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by trusting order without becoming passive, and doing your dharma while leaving total control to the Lord.

Story: Rama defends the sages of the forest and keeps his father's word despite hardship; this name honors steadfast protective dharma.

507 puruṣattamaḥ पुरुषत्तमः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Best of persons.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

508 vinayaḥ विनयः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Discipline and humility.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

509 jayaḥ जयः Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Victory.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an affirmation that the ruling Lord is not inert principle but conscious governance present in the moral order.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by asking where divine protection in your life should become ethical steadiness rather than mere emotional comfort.

Story: Vishnu destroys Madhu and Kaitabha when cosmic order is threatened; the story shows protection at the scale of creation itself.

510 satyasaṅdhaḥ सत्यसन्धः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: True to his promise.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

511 dāśārhaḥ दाशार्हः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of the Yadava line.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

512 sātvataṁ patiḥ सात्वतां पतिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Lord of the Satvatas.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

513 jīvaḥ जीवः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Life principle in all.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

514 vinayitāsākṣī विनयितासाक्षी Yoga, inwardness, and steadiness

Meaning: Witness of the disciplined.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a direct support for inwardness, witness-awareness, and mind-gathering contemplation.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by bringing the mind back to witnessing whenever it becomes overrun by reaction.

Story: Kapila's instruction to Devahuti joins analysis and devotion, making this name shine as inner clarity.

515 mukundaḥ मुकुन्दः Freedom, destination, and moksha

Meaning: Giver of liberation.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a direct pointer toward moksha as knowledge of reality rather than as a mere improved condition inside samsara.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by asking what you still take to be absolutely necessary even though it cannot survive change.

Story: Dhruva seeks a kingdom but receives direct vision and a transformed heart; this name matches the Lord who turns ambition toward liberation.

516 amitavikramaḥ अमितविक्रमः Rama-centered names

Meaning: Of boundless stride and valor.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that avatara names are not metaphysical limitations of Brahman but compassionate manifestations for seekers.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by allowing the story to hold the heart steady until deeper understanding becomes possible.

Story: The reunion with Bharata displays a form of sovereignty anchored in restraint rather than possession.

517 ambhonidhiḥ अम्भोनिधिः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Ocean, repository of waters.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that cosmic texture is not independent substance but appearance upheld in consciousness.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by observing change in the world while remembering the changeless that makes change knowable.

Story: Matsya guides the ark through the deluge, showing the Lord present in waters both destructive and protective.

518 anantātmā अनन्तात्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of infinite self.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

519 mahodadhiśayaḥ महोदधिशयः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: He who reclines on the great ocean.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a contemplative hint that what seems manifold in nature rests in one unfragmented truth.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by allowing elemental experience to point beyond itself toward the one reality it depends on.

Story: Krishna dances upon Kaliya in the Yamuna, purifying poisoned waters; this name aligns with divine mastery within nature.

520 antakaḥ अन्तकः Time, rhythm, and ordinance

Meaning: Ender of all at the right time.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to seeing that calendar, causation, and destiny belong to vyavahara, not to the absolute as such.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by remembering that what changes in time is not the deepest self.

Story: Bhishma himself waits for the auspicious time of uttarayana before departing, a fitting narrative backdrop for names of divine timing and order.

521 ajaḥ अजः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Unborn.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

522 mahārhaḥ महार्हः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Supremely worthy of worship.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

523 svābhāvyaḥ स्वाभाव्यः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Existing by his own nature.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

524 jitāmitraḥ जितामित्रः Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Conqueror of foes.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a saguna-Ishvara name through which grace, order, and dharmic correction become meditatively approachable.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by trusting order without becoming passive, and doing your dharma while leaving total control to the Lord.

Story: Krishna breaks Kamsa's reign after years of hidden preparation; this name reflects a protection that can be patient before it becomes dramatic.

525 pramodanaḥ प्रमोदनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Giver of joy.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

526 ānandaḥ आनन्दः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Bliss itself.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

527 nandanaḥ नन्दनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The delightful one.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

528 nandaḥ नन्दः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Joy.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

529 satyadharmā सत्यधर्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Whose dharma is true.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

530 trivikramaḥ त्रिविक्रमः Vamana and Trivikrama names

Meaning: He of the three strides.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a form-centered praise that steadies devotion while quietly training the mind toward the limitless reality behind form.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using personal devotion as the emotional purification that prepares the mind for knowledge.

Story: Bali's surrender of even his own head under Vamana's request reveals the Lord as purifier of generosity.

531 maharṣiḥ kapilācāryaḥ महर्षिः कपिलाचार्यः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The great sage Kapila.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

532 kṛtajñaḥ कृतज्ञः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Knower of every act and gratitude.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a revelation of Vishnu as witness-consciousness and as the light by which scripture and experience become intelligible.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by joining study, self-inquiry, and devotion instead of separating them into different compartments.

Story: Narada repeatedly carries divine insight into confused situations, and his guidance ultimately points back to Vishnu; this name aligns with such illuminating grace.

533 medinīpatiḥ मेदिनीपतिः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Lord of the earth.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a way of reading the elements as nama-rupa dependent on one underlying reality.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by turning sky, light, wind, and water into meditation prompts instead of background scenery.

Story: Govardhana itself becomes a sign of worship when Krishna turns the community toward the mountain, cows, and rain that sustain life.

534 tripadaḥ त्रिपदः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He whose stride is threefold.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

535 tridaśādhyakṣaḥ त्रिदशाध्यक्षः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Overseer of the devas.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a name that links devotion to viveka, because the Lord is both the revealer of truth and the ground of knowing.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by asking not only what is happening, but who is the knower in whose light the happening is known.

Story: Akrura's pilgrimage to meet Krishna transforms expectation into revelation; this name fits wisdom that descends as vision, not abstraction alone.

536 mahāśṛṅgaḥ महाशृङ्गः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Great-horned; fish or boar-like rescuing form.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

537 kṛtāntakṛt कृतान्तकृत् Time, rhythm, and ordinance

Meaning: Maker of the end of time or death.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to seeing that calendar, causation, and destiny belong to vyavahara, not to the absolute as such.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by remembering that what changes in time is not the deepest self.

Story: Rama's exile ripens many destinies through disciplined endurance; this name suits a Lord whose order unfolds through season and patience.

538 mahāvarāhaḥ महावराहः Varaha-centered names

Meaning: The great boar incarnation.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a form-centered praise that steadies devotion while quietly training the mind toward the limitless reality behind form.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using personal devotion as the emotional purification that prepares the mind for knowledge.

Story: Varaha dives into the cosmic waters and raises Bhudevi on his tusks; earth-related names naturally resonate with this act.

539 govindaḥ गोविन्दः Krishna-centered names

Meaning: Finder and protector of cows, earth, and senses.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a form-centered praise that steadies devotion while quietly training the mind toward the limitless reality behind form.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using personal devotion as the emotional purification that prepares the mind for knowledge.

Story: The rescue of the boys from the forest fire reveals quiet divine guardianship amid play.

540 suṣeṇaḥ सुषेणः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He of noble armies.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

541 kanakāṅgadī कनकाङ्गदी Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Adorned with golden armlets.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

542 guhyaḥ गुह्यः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Secret, mysterious.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

543 gabhīraḥ गभीरः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Deep and profound.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

544 gahanaḥ गहनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Hard to fathom.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

545 guptaḥ गुप्तः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Hidden, protected.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

546 cakragadādharaḥ चक्रगदाधरः Weapons, emblems, and corrective power

Meaning: Bearer of discus and mace.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that even martial imagery can serve Advaita by depicting the destruction of misapprehension.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by treating discipline as grace in active form.

Story: The Sharnga bow in many Purana narratives symbolizes readiness to protect without hesitation; this name extends that image.

547 vedhāḥ वेधाः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Creator.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

548 svāṅgaḥ स्वाङ्गः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Whose limbs are perfect and self-sufficient.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

549 ajitaḥ अजितः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Unconquered.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

550 kṛṣṇaḥ कृष्णः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The dark and all-attractive one.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

551 dṛḍhaḥ दृढः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Firm and steadfast.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

552 saṅkarṣaṇo'cyutaḥ सङ्कर्षणोऽच्युतः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Sankarshana, the infallible one.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

553 varuṇaḥ वरुणः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Lord of waters and moral depth.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that cosmic texture is not independent substance but appearance upheld in consciousness.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by observing change in the world while remembering the changeless that makes change knowable.

Story: The descent of the Ganga through divine mediation reflects a cosmos where sacred waters carry grace into the world.

554 vāruṇaḥ वारुणः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Related to the watery power.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that cosmic texture is not independent substance but appearance upheld in consciousness.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by observing change in the world while remembering the changeless that makes change knowable.

Story: The serpent Ananta becomes Vishnu's couch, revealing even immense natural force as restful service in the divine presence.

555 vṛkṣaḥ वृक्षः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: The tree, the support of life.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a contemplative hint that what seems manifold in nature rests in one unfragmented truth.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by allowing elemental experience to point beyond itself toward the one reality it depends on.

Story: Garuda's service shows sky, speed, and bird-power offered wholly to the Lord; this name resonates with that elevation.

556 puṣkarākṣaḥ पुष्कराक्षः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Lotus-eyed.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a name in which Sri becomes a contemplative symbol of harmony, grace, and all-sided completeness.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by redefining prosperity as inward clarity plus wise stewardship of what has been given.

Story: In Tirumala lore, Srinivasa receives and protects the wealth of devotion offered by countless pilgrims; such narratives illuminate auspicious names.

557 mahāmanāḥ महामनाः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Great-minded.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

558 bhagavān भगवान् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The possessor of all divine excellences.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

559 bhagahā भगहा Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Destroyer of false prosperity.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a saguna-Ishvara name through which grace, order, and dharmic correction become meditatively approachable.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by trusting order without becoming passive, and doing your dharma while leaving total control to the Lord.

Story: When Banasura's arrogance overwhelms his own good fortune, Krishna defeats him without needless cruelty; this name shows protective justice with restraint.

560 ānandī आनन्दी Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Joyful and joy-giving.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

561 vanamālī वनमाली Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Wearer of the forest garland.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

562 halāyudhaḥ हलायुधः Balarama-centered names

Meaning: Wielder of the plough.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that avatara names are not metaphysical limitations of Brahman but compassionate manifestations for seekers.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by allowing the story to hold the heart steady until deeper understanding becomes possible.

Story: Balarama's plough-bearing strength symbolizes grounded power that prepares and protects; names linked to Halayudha fit this steady might.

563 ādityaḥ आदित्यः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Son of Aditi, the radiant one.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

564 jyotirādityaḥ ज्योतिरादित्यः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Solar splendor of light.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

565 sahiṣṇuḥ सहिष्णुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Forbearing.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.
