Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

566 gatisattamaḥ गतिसत्तमः Freedom, destination, and moksha

Meaning: The highest goal and movement.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a contemplative invitation to move from devotional dependence to liberating recognition without discarding devotion.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by remembering that devotion need not stop at consolation; it can culminate in liberating knowledge.

Story: Gajendra's rescue is not merely escape from a crocodile but deliverance from bondage and forgetfulness; this epithet fits that deeper release.

567 sudhanvā सुधन्वा Weapons, emblems, and corrective power

Meaning: Of excellent bow.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a symbolic praise of the Lord's power to cut avidya, protect dharma, and remove inner obstruction.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by letting discrimination cut confusion before it grows into habit.

Story: Panchajanya, Krishna's conch, announces righteous action and awakens hearts before battle; the name reflects sound as sacred power.

568 khaṇḍaparaśuḥ खण्डपरशुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Wielder of the battle-axe.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

569 dāruṇaḥ दारुणः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Stern and formidable to evil.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

570 draviṇapradaḥ द्रविणप्रदः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Giver of wealth and resources.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

571 divaḥspṛk दिवःस्पृक् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Touching the heavens.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

572 sarvadṛgvyāsaḥ सर्वदृग्व्यासः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: The all-seeing arranger of knowledge.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a name that links devotion to viveka, because the Lord is both the revealer of truth and the ground of knowing.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by asking not only what is happening, but who is the knower in whose light the happening is known.

Story: Sanatkumara and the sages seek the supreme truth and find it centered in Narayana; the name suits the Lord who is both teacher and taught-of.

573 vācaspatirayonijaḥ वाचस्पतिरयोनिजः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Lord of speech, not born from a womb.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a contemplative bridge from sacred word to direct recognition of the self-luminous reality.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by letting the name turn curiosity into discrimination and discrimination into stillness.

Story: In the Gajendra episode, the elephant's past-life memory of prayer returns at the crucial moment; this name reflects divine knowledge awakening dormant wisdom.

574 trisāmā त्रिसामा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Essence of the three Sama chants.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

575 sāmagaḥ सामगः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Singer of the Sama.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

576 sāma साम Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: The Sama Veda itself, harmony.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a revelation of Vishnu as witness-consciousness and as the light by which scripture and experience become intelligible.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by joining study, self-inquiry, and devotion instead of separating them into different compartments.

Story: Krishna instructs Arjuna on the battlefield, turning despair into discernment; this name belongs to the Lord who teaches inside crisis.

577 nirvāṇam निर्वाणम् Freedom, destination, and moksha

Meaning: Supreme peace and release.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a direct pointer toward moksha as knowledge of reality rather than as a mere improved condition inside samsara.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by asking what you still take to be absolutely necessary even though it cannot survive change.

Story: Ajamila's last-minute remembrance opens a path beyond the mechanics of punishment; this name points to grace beyond despair.

578 bheṣajam भेषजम् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The medicine.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

579 bhiṣak भिषक् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The healer.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

580 saṁnyāsakṛt संन्यासकृत् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Giver or institutor of renunciation.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

581 śamaḥ शमः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Inner quietude.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

582 śāntaḥ शान्तः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Peaceful.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

583 niṣṭhā निष्ठा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Steady foundation.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

584 śāntiḥ शान्तिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Peace itself.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

585 parāyaṇam परायणम् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The supreme refuge.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

586 śubhāṅgaḥ शुभाङ्गः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Of auspicious limbs.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a praise of auspicious fullness in Ishvara: beauty and order serving as supports for contemplation.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by keeping outer order, gratitude, and cleanliness so the mind becomes fit for sacred attention.

Story: Rukmini's heart turns unwaveringly toward Krishna before their marriage; this name reflects divine beauty that draws love by its own worth.

587 śāntidaḥ शान्तिदः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Giver of peace.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

588 sraṣṭā स्रष्टा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Creator.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

589 kumudaḥ कुमुदः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Delighter of the earth.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that cosmic texture is not independent substance but appearance upheld in consciousness.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by observing change in the world while remembering the changeless that makes change knowable.

Story: The moonlike cooling of Krishna's presence in Vraja often follows scenes of heat and turmoil; such names fit the soothing aspect of divinity in nature.

590 kuvaleśayaḥ कुवलेशयः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: He who rests on the waters or within the lotus-world.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that sacred radiance is not opposed to truth but can refine the mind toward it.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by seeing beauty as a support for recollection rather than as a distraction to be resented.

Story: When Sudama visits Krishna in poverty and returns to quiet abundance, the story shows auspicious grace arriving without humiliation.

591 gohitaḥ गोहितः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Well-wisher of cows, earth, and beings.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a contemplative hint that what seems manifold in nature rests in one unfragmented truth.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by allowing elemental experience to point beyond itself toward the one reality it depends on.

Story: In the forest episodes of the Ramayana, trees, rivers, and mountains become witnesses to Rama's dharma; this name turns the landscape into theology.

592 gopatiḥ गोपतिः Krishna-centered names

Meaning: Lord of cows and protectees.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a form-centered praise that steadies devotion while quietly training the mind toward the limitless reality behind form.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using personal devotion as the emotional purification that prepares the mind for knowledge.

Story: Brahma's bewilderment in Vraja shows Krishna's supremacy even in apparently ordinary village life.

593 goptā गोप्ता Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Protector.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a support for devotion to the Lord who removes obstacles to knowledge by preserving righteousness.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by seeing the removal of confusion as one of the Lord's most important forms of protection.

Story: In saving the elephant king, Vishnu accepts no delay because the cry is sincere; that immediacy illustrates this name.

594 vṛṣabhākṣaḥ वृषभाक्षः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Bull-eyed, or whose vision is dharma.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

595 vṛṣapriyaḥ वृषप्रियः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Lover of righteousness.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

596 anivartī अनिवर्ती Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Never retreating.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

597 nivṛttātmā निवृत्तात्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Whose self is detached from worldly compulsion.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

598 saṁkṣeptā संक्षेप्ता Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He who contracts the universe.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

599 kṣemakṛt क्षेमकृत् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Maker of welfare and security.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

600 śivaḥ शिवः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Auspicious and pure.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a name in which Sri becomes a contemplative symbol of harmony, grace, and all-sided completeness.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by redefining prosperity as inward clarity plus wise stewardship of what has been given.

Story: The Lord seated with Sri on his chest symbolizes a kingship inseparable from compassion and harmony; this name points to that inseparable splendor.

Names 601-700

601 śrīvatsavakṣāḥ श्रीवत्सवक्षाः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: He whose chest bears the Śrīvatsa mark.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a name in which Sri becomes a contemplative symbol of harmony, grace, and all-sided completeness.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by redefining prosperity as inward clarity plus wise stewardship of what has been given.

Story: Tulasi's devotion becomes eternally joined to Vishnu's worship, showing that auspiciousness includes purity of offering and love.

602 śrīvāsaḥ श्रीवासः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Abode of Sri.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a name in which Sri becomes a contemplative symbol of harmony, grace, and all-sided completeness.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by redefining prosperity as inward clarity plus wise stewardship of what has been given.

Story: The Samudra Manthana produces many treasures, yet Lakshmi's choosing of Vishnu reveals where true prosperity ultimately rests.

603 śrīpatiḥ श्रीपतिः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Lord of Sri.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a praise of auspicious fullness in Ishvara: beauty and order serving as supports for contemplation.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by keeping outer order, gratitude, and cleanliness so the mind becomes fit for sacred attention.

Story: In the story of Andal's longing for Ranganatha, divine beauty becomes an object of total surrender; this name resonates with that magnetic grace.

604 śrīmatāṁ varaḥ श्रीमतां वरः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Best among the glorious.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a praise of auspicious fullness in Ishvara: beauty and order serving as supports for contemplation.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by keeping outer order, gratitude, and cleanliness so the mind becomes fit for sacred attention.

Story: At the emergence of Lakshmi from the milk ocean, she chooses Vishnu as her eternal abode; this name naturally carries the glow of that union.

605 śrīdaḥ श्रीदः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Giver of prosperity and grace.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a praise of auspicious fullness in Ishvara: beauty and order serving as supports for contemplation.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by keeping outer order, gratitude, and cleanliness so the mind becomes fit for sacred attention.

Story: In Tirumala lore, Srinivasa receives and protects the wealth of devotion offered by countless pilgrims; such narratives illuminate auspicious names.

606 śrīśaḥ श्रीशः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Lord of Sri.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that sacred radiance is not opposed to truth but can refine the mind toward it.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by seeing beauty as a support for recollection rather than as a distraction to be resented.

Story: Rukmini's heart turns unwaveringly toward Krishna before their marriage; this name reflects divine beauty that draws love by its own worth.

607 śrīnivāsaḥ श्रीनिवासः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Abode of Sri.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that sacred radiance is not opposed to truth but can refine the mind toward it.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by seeing beauty as a support for recollection rather than as a distraction to be resented.

Story: When Sudama visits Krishna in poverty and returns to quiet abundance, the story shows auspicious grace arriving without humiliation.

608 śrīnidhiḥ श्रीनिधिः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Treasure of Sri.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that sacred radiance is not opposed to truth but can refine the mind toward it.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by seeing beauty as a support for recollection rather than as a distraction to be resented.

Story: The Lord seated with Sri on his chest symbolizes a kingship inseparable from compassion and harmony; this name points to that inseparable splendor.

609 śrīvibhāvanaḥ श्रीविभावनः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Manifestor of auspicious prosperity.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a name in which Sri becomes a contemplative symbol of harmony, grace, and all-sided completeness.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by redefining prosperity as inward clarity plus wise stewardship of what has been given.

Story: Tulasi's devotion becomes eternally joined to Vishnu's worship, showing that auspiciousness includes purity of offering and love.

610 śrīdharaḥ श्रीधरः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Bearer of Sri.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a name in which Sri becomes a contemplative symbol of harmony, grace, and all-sided completeness.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by redefining prosperity as inward clarity plus wise stewardship of what has been given.

Story: The Samudra Manthana produces many treasures, yet Lakshmi's choosing of Vishnu reveals where true prosperity ultimately rests.

611 śrīkaraḥ श्रीकरः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Maker of auspiciousness.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a name in which Sri becomes a contemplative symbol of harmony, grace, and all-sided completeness.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by redefining prosperity as inward clarity plus wise stewardship of what has been given.

Story: In the story of Andal's longing for Ranganatha, divine beauty becomes an object of total surrender; this name resonates with that magnetic grace.

612 śreyaḥ श्रेयः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The highest good.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

613 śrīmān श्रीमान् Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Ever endowed with Sri.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a praise of auspicious fullness in Ishvara: beauty and order serving as supports for contemplation.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by keeping outer order, gratitude, and cleanliness so the mind becomes fit for sacred attention.

Story: At the emergence of Lakshmi from the milk ocean, she chooses Vishnu as her eternal abode; this name naturally carries the glow of that union.

614 lokatrayāśrayaḥ लोकत्रयाश्रयः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Refuge of the three worlds.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

615 svakṣaḥ स्वक्षः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of beautiful eyes.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

616 svaṅgaḥ स्वङ्गः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of perfect limbs.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

617 śatānandaḥ शतानन्दः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of hundredfold joy.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

618 nandiḥ नन्दिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The rejoicing one.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

619 jyotirgaṇeśvaraḥ ज्योतिर्गणेश्वरः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Lord of all luminaries.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

620 vijitātmā विजितात्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Master of himself.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

621 vidheyātmā विधेयात्मा Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Whose self is obedient to truth, or who makes beings disciplined.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a revelation of Vishnu as witness-consciousness and as the light by which scripture and experience become intelligible.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by joining study, self-inquiry, and devotion instead of separating them into different compartments.

Story: As Hayagriva, Vishnu restores the Vedas after they are stolen; the story suits names tied to knowledge and sacred speech.

622 satkīrtiḥ सत्कीर्तिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of noble fame.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

623 chinnasaṁśayaḥ छिन्नसंशयः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Cutter of doubt.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

624 udīrṇaḥ उदीर्णः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Lofty and exalted.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

625 sarvataścakṣuḥ सर्वतश्चक्षुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Eyes on every side.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

626 anīśaḥ अनीशः Time, rhythm, and ordinance

Meaning: None rules over him.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that the Lord presides over temporal process while remaining untouched by its mutations.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name when restless about results; it helps distinguish eternal value from temporary sequence.

Story: Vamana's measured three steps transform cosmic space into sacred order; this epithet reflects the Lord as measurer and ordainer.

627 śāśvatasthiraḥ शाश्वतस्थिरः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Eternally stable.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

628 bhūśayaḥ भूशयः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: He who reclines on the earth or in the hearts of beings.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a contemplative hint that what seems manifold in nature rests in one unfragmented truth.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by allowing elemental experience to point beyond itself toward the one reality it depends on.

Story: Varaha lifting the earth from cosmic waters makes elemental imagery literal; this name suits Vishnu as guardian of the world-body.

629 bhūṣaṇaḥ भूषणः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Ornament of the world.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

630 bhūtiḥ भूतिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Prosperity and glory.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

631 viśokaḥ विशोकः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Free from sorrow.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

632 śokanāśanaḥ शोकनाशनः Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Destroyer of sorrow.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a saguna-Ishvara name through which grace, order, and dharmic correction become meditatively approachable.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by trusting order without becoming passive, and doing your dharma while leaving total control to the Lord.

Story: The Sudarshana protects Ambarisha from Durvasa's fury, proving this name through preservation of the humble devotee.

633 arciṣmān अर्चिष्मान् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Effulgent with flames and rays.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

634 arcitaḥ अर्चितः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Worshipped.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

635 kumbhaḥ कुम्भः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The vessel, fullness.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

636 viśuddhātmā विशुद्धात्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of utterly pure self.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

637 viśodhanaḥ विशोधनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The purifier.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

638 aniruddhaḥ अनिरुद्धः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Unobstructed; the emanation Aniruddha.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

639 apratirathaḥ अप्रतिरथः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Without an equal opponent in battle.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

640 pradyumnaḥ प्रद्युम्नः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Splendidly luminous; the emanation Pradyumna.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

641 amitavikramaḥ अमितविक्रमः Rama-centered names

Meaning: Of immeasurable valor.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an upasana-rich name: form is honored as a valid doorway through which the mind approaches the formless truth.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by meditating on the form lovingly while remembering that the form is a doorway, not a boundary.

Story: Rama's exile keeps truth to his father's word above comfort, making righteous names shine with moral gravity.

642 kālaneminihā कालनेमिनिहा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Slayer of Kālanemi.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

643 vīraḥ वीरः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The hero.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

644 śauriḥ शौरिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The heroic one, of the Śūra line.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

645 śūrajaneśvaraḥ शूरजनेश्वरः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Lord of the heroic people.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

646 trilokātmā त्रिलोकात्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Soul of the three worlds.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

647 trilokeśaḥ त्रिलोकेशः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Lord of the three worlds.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

648 keśavaḥ केशवः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Beautiful-haired one; slayer of Keshi.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a way of reading the elements as nama-rupa dependent on one underlying reality.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by turning sky, light, wind, and water into meditation prompts instead of background scenery.

Story: Kurma supports Mount Mandara during the churning, turning mountain and ocean alike into instruments of divine purpose.

649 keśihā केशिहा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Slayer of the Keshi demon.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

650 hariḥ हरिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He who removes bondage and sin.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

651 kāmadevaḥ कामदेवः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Lord of love.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

652 kāmapālaḥ कामपालः Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Protector of rightful desire.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an affirmation that the ruling Lord is not inert principle but conscious governance present in the moral order.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by asking where divine protection in your life should become ethical steadiness rather than mere emotional comfort.

Story: Krishna safeguards Parikshit in the womb from Ashvatthama's weapon; the name fits that unseen guardianship of future dharma.

653 kāmī कामी Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He who is full in himself yet grants loving fulfilment.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

654 kāntaḥ कान्तः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The beloved and beautiful one.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

655 kṛtāgamaḥ कृतागमः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Source of the sacred traditions.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

656 anirdeśyavapuḥ अनिर्देश्यवपुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of indescribable form.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

657 viṣṇuḥ विष्णुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The all-pervading one.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

658 vīraḥ वीरः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The hero.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

659 anantaḥ अनन्तः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Endless, infinite.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

660 dhanañjayaḥ धनञ्जयः Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Winner of wealth and victory.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an affirmation that the ruling Lord is not inert principle but conscious governance present in the moral order.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by asking where divine protection in your life should become ethical steadiness rather than mere emotional comfort.

Story: As Mohini, Vishnu protects the devas by recovering the amrita from a chaotic scramble; this name reflects strategic preservation.

661 brahmaṇyaḥ ब्रह्मण्यः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Protector of sacred wisdom and the brahmanical order.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a name that links devotion to viveka, because the Lord is both the revealer of truth and the ground of knowing.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by asking not only what is happening, but who is the knower in whose light the happening is known.

Story: Kapila teaches Devahuti the way of liberation and right seeing; this name resonates with Vishnu as clarifying intelligence.

662 brahmakṛt ब्रह्मकृत् Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: Maker of spiritual knowledge and sacred order.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that ritual is fulfilled when it matures into inner offering and knowledge of the divine center.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by treating daily action as worship that should purify the mind, not merely accumulate results.

Story: The Rajasuya of Yudhishthira culminates in Krishna receiving first honors, showing that all offerings find their center in him.

663 brahmā ब्रह्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The creator principle.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

664 brahma ब्रह्म Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The absolute reality.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

665 brahmavivardhanaḥ ब्रह्मविवर्धनः Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: Promoter of spiritual wisdom.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an Upasana-friendly name that pulls outward worship back toward the one inner receiver of all sacred action.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by remembering that the deepest oblation is ego-centeredness itself.

Story: Daksha's disrupted sacrifice is restored only when the deeper principle of divine order is honored; this name suits Vishnu as the true sanctifier of ritual.

666 brahmavit ब्रह्मवित् Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Knower of Brahman.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a revelation of Vishnu as witness-consciousness and as the light by which scripture and experience become intelligible.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by joining study, self-inquiry, and devotion instead of separating them into different compartments.

Story: Uddhava receives Krishna's final teachings at the end of the Yadava era; this epithet shines in wisdom given before departure.

667 brāhmaṇaḥ ब्राह्मणः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: He who is steeped in sacred wisdom.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a revelation of Vishnu as witness-consciousness and as the light by which scripture and experience become intelligible.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by joining study, self-inquiry, and devotion instead of separating them into different compartments.

Story: The child Dhruva receives mantra and then direct vision, showing that true knowledge matures from remembrance into realization.

668 brahmī ब्रह्मी Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Embodied in Brahmic wisdom.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a revelation of Vishnu as witness-consciousness and as the light by which scripture and experience become intelligible.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by joining study, self-inquiry, and devotion instead of separating them into different compartments.

Story: When Brahma is humbled by Krishna's play in Vraja, he gains clearer understanding of the Lord's inconceivable nature; this name fits that awakening.

669 brahmajñaḥ ब्रह्मज्ञः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Knower of the absolute.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a name that links devotion to viveka, because the Lord is both the revealer of truth and the ground of knowing.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by asking not only what is happening, but who is the knower in whose light the happening is known.

Story: Narada repeatedly carries divine insight into confused situations, and his guidance ultimately points back to Vishnu; this name aligns with such illuminating grace.

670 brāhmaṇapriyaḥ ब्राह्मणप्रियः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Dear to seekers of sacred knowledge.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a name that links devotion to viveka, because the Lord is both the revealer of truth and the ground of knowing.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by asking not only what is happening, but who is the knower in whose light the happening is known.

Story: Akrura's pilgrimage to meet Krishna transforms expectation into revelation; this name fits wisdom that descends as vision, not abstraction alone.

671 mahākramaḥ महाक्रमः Rama-centered names

Meaning: Of great stride.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that avatara names are not metaphysical limitations of Brahman but compassionate manifestations for seekers.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by allowing the story to hold the heart steady until deeper understanding becomes possible.

Story: The bridge to Lanka shows leadership that gathers many beings into one dharmic purpose.

672 mahākarmā महाकर्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Doer of great acts.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

673 mahātejāḥ महातेजाः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of great splendor.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

674 mahoragaḥ महोरगः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Great serpent-like cosmic power.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a contemplative hint that what seems manifold in nature rests in one unfragmented truth.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by allowing elemental experience to point beyond itself toward the one reality it depends on.

Story: Matsya guides the ark through the deluge, showing the Lord present in waters both destructive and protective.

675 mahākratuḥ महाक्रतुः Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: The great sacrifice.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a way of seeing sacrifice, offerer, offering, and fruit as resting in one Brahman through Ishvara.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by offering speech, labor, food, and restraint inwardly to the Lord rather than performing them with vanity.

Story: When Krishna teaches that offerings made with devotion are accepted even if simple, this epithet becomes inward and universal.

676 mahāyajvā महायज्वा Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: Great performer of sacrifice.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a way of seeing sacrifice, offerer, offering, and fruit as resting in one Brahman through Ishvara.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by offering speech, labor, food, and restraint inwardly to the Lord rather than performing them with vanity.

Story: Bali's gift to Vamana turns a ritual act into total surrender; this name fits the Lord who receives both offering and offerer.

677 mahāyajñaḥ महायज्ञः Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: The great sacrifice itself.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a way of seeing sacrifice, offerer, offering, and fruit as resting in one Brahman through Ishvara.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by offering speech, labor, food, and restraint inwardly to the Lord rather than performing them with vanity.

Story: The sages at Naimisharanya keep sacrificial time alive through remembrance of Narayana; the name belongs to the divine heart of ritual continuity.

678 mahāhaviḥ महाहविः Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: The great oblation.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that ritual is fulfilled when it matures into inner offering and knowledge of the divine center.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by treating daily action as worship that should purify the mind, not merely accumulate results.

Story: In Ambarisha's observance of Ekadashi and Dvadashi, discipline itself becomes worship; this name suits Vishnu as the fulfiller of vowed offering.

679 stavyaḥ स्तव्यः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Worthy of praise.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

680 stavapriyaḥ स्तवप्रियः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Lover of praise.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

681 stotram स्तोत्रम् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The hymn itself.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

682 stutiḥ स्तुतिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Praise.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

683 stotā स्तोता Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The praiser.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

684 raṇapriyaḥ रणप्रियः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Lover of righteous battle.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

685 pūrṇaḥ पूर्णः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He who is complete and perfect.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

686 pūrayitā पूरयिता Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The fulfiller.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

687 puṇyaḥ पुण्यः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Holy and meritorious.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

688 puṇyakīrtiḥ पुण्यकीर्तिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of sacred fame.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

689 anāmayaḥ अनामयः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Free from affliction.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

690 manojavaḥ मनोजवः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Swift as the mind.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

691 tīrthakaraḥ तीर्थकरः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Maker of sacred crossings.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

692 vasuretāḥ वसुरेताः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Whose seed is abundance and light.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

693 vasupradaḥ वसुप्रदः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Giver of wealth.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

694 vasupradaḥ वसुप्रदः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Bestower again of treasure.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

695 vāsudevaḥ वासुदेवः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The indwelling Lord in all; son of Vasudeva.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

696 vasuḥ वसुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The indwelling treasure.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

697 vasumanāḥ वसुमनाः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of generous and noble mind.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

698 haviḥ हविः Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: The oblation.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that ritual is fulfilled when it matures into inner offering and knowledge of the divine center.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by treating daily action as worship that should purify the mind, not merely accumulate results.

Story: In the churning of the ocean, gods and asuras labor for nectar, but the whole enterprise reaches order only through Vishnu's oversight; this name shows the Lord as the hidden receiver of sacrifice.

699 sadgatiḥ सद्गतिः Freedom, destination, and moksha

Meaning: The good goal.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a contemplative invitation to move from devotional dependence to liberating recognition without discarding devotion.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by remembering that devotion need not stop at consolation; it can culminate in liberating knowledge.

Story: The Four Kumaras attain devotion through the fragrance of Tulasi at Vishnu's feet, showing liberation softened into loving nearness.

700 satkṛtiḥ सत्कृतिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of noble action and honor.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

Names 701-800

701 sattā सत्ता Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Pure being.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

702 sadbhūtiḥ सद्भूतिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: True prosperity and existence.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

703 satparāyaṇaḥ सत्परायणः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Supreme refuge of the good.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

704 śūrasenaḥ शूरसेनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of the Sura or Yadu line.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

705 yaduśreṣṭhaḥ यदुश्रेष्ठः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Best of the Yadus.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

706 sannivāsaḥ सन्निवासः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Abode of the noble.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

707 suyāmunaḥ सुयामुनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Blessed by the Yamuna region.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

708 bhūtāvāsaḥ भूतावासः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Abode of all beings.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.
