709 vāsudevaḥ वासुदेवः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The indwelling Lord in all.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

710 sarvāsunilayaḥ सर्वासुनिलयः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Abode of all life-breaths.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a contemplative hint that what seems manifold in nature rests in one unfragmented truth.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by allowing elemental experience to point beyond itself toward the one reality it depends on.

Story: Krishna dances upon Kaliya in the Yamuna, purifying poisoned waters; this name aligns with divine mastery within nature.

711 analaḥ अनलः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Fire, endless energy.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a way of reading the elements as nama-rupa dependent on one underlying reality.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by turning sky, light, wind, and water into meditation prompts instead of background scenery.

Story: Govardhana itself becomes a sign of worship when Krishna turns the community toward the mountain, cows, and rain that sustain life.

712 darpahā दर्पहा Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Destroyer of pride.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a saguna-Ishvara name through which grace, order, and dharmic correction become meditatively approachable.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by trusting order without becoming passive, and doing your dharma while leaving total control to the Lord.

Story: Narasimha bursts forth to save Prahlada and break tyrannical pride; this name fits the Lord whose protection arrives exactly where hope seems blocked.

713 darpadaḥ दर्पदः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Giver of healthy self-respect and power.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

714 dṛptaḥ दृप्तः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Majestic and confident.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

715 durdharaḥ दुर्धरः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Difficult to hold or restrain.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

716 athāparājitaḥ अथापराजितः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The unsurpassed, never defeated one.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

717 viśvamūrtiḥ विश्वमूर्तिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Whose form is the whole universe.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

718 mahāmūrtiḥ महामूर्तिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of immense form.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

719 dīptamūrtiḥ दीप्तमूर्तिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of radiant form.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

720 amūrtimān अमूर्तिमान् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Formless yet possessing all forms.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

721 anekamūrtiḥ अनेकमूर्तिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of many forms.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

722 avyaktaḥ अव्यक्तः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The unmanifest.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

723 śatamūrtiḥ शतमूर्तिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Hundred-formed.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

724 śatānanaḥ शताननः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Hundred-faced.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

725 ekaḥ एकः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The one.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

726 naikaḥ नैकः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The many.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

727 savaḥ सवः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The sacrificial current.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

728 kaḥ कः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The blissful one, the divine interrogative.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

729 kim किम् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: What, the object of inquiry.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

730 yat यत् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: That which is.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

731 tat तत् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: That supreme reality.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

732 padam-anuttamam पदमनुत्तमम् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The unsurpassed state.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

733 lokabandhuḥ लोकबन्धुः Compassion, refuge, and tenderness

Meaning: Friend of the world.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that in Advaita too, devotion and divine responsiveness are not bypassed but deepened.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name when you feel impure or unready; it reminds the heart that purification is possible.

Story: When Krishna forgives many offenses before finally disciplining Shishupala, the balance of patience and justice illuminates this name.

734 lokanāthaḥ लोकनाथः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Lord of the world.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

735 mādhavaḥ माधवः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Lord of Sri and of wisdom.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a name in which Sri becomes a contemplative symbol of harmony, grace, and all-sided completeness.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by redefining prosperity as inward clarity plus wise stewardship of what has been given.

Story: In Tirumala lore, Srinivasa receives and protects the wealth of devotion offered by countless pilgrims; such narratives illuminate auspicious names.

736 bhaktavatsalaḥ भक्तवत्सलः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Tenderly affectionate to devotees.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

737 suvarṇavarṇaḥ सुवर्णवर्णः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Golden-hued.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

738 hemāṅgaḥ हेमाङ्गः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Golden-limbed.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

739 varāṅgaḥ वराङ्गः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of beautiful limbs.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

740 candanāṅgadī चन्दनाङ्गदी Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Adorned with sandal and armlets.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

741 vīrahā वीरहा Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Destroyer of opposing heroes.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an affirmation that the ruling Lord is not inert principle but conscious governance present in the moral order.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by asking where divine protection in your life should become ethical steadiness rather than mere emotional comfort.

Story: Krishna lifts Govardhana to shelter Vraja from Indra's storm; this name belongs to divine strength expressed as refuge.

742 viṣamaḥ विषमः Time, rhythm, and ordinance

Meaning: Incomparable and beyond ordinary measure.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that the Lord presides over temporal process while remaining untouched by its mutations.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name when restless about results; it helps distinguish eternal value from temporary sequence.

Story: The Sudarshana chakra itself symbolizes time cutting through disorder; the story of Ambarisha and Durvasa gives this image moral force.

743 śūnyaḥ शून्यः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Empty of limitation, beyond material definition.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that cosmic texture is not independent substance but appearance upheld in consciousness.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by observing change in the world while remembering the changeless that makes change knowable.

Story: The descent of the Ganga through divine mediation reflects a cosmos where sacred waters carry grace into the world.

744 ghṛtāśīḥ घृताशीः Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: He who accepts clarified offerings.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an Upasana-friendly name that pulls outward worship back toward the one inner receiver of all sacred action.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by remembering that the deepest oblation is ego-centeredness itself.

Story: As Yajna in primordial times, Vishnu upholds cosmic order through sacrificial harmony; the name fits the deity who is both rite and fruit.

745 acalaḥ अचलः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Immovable.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

746 calaḥ चलः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Moving through all.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

747 amānī अमानी Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Free from egoistic demand for honor.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

748 mānadaḥ मानदः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Giver of honor.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

749 mānyaḥ मान्यः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Worthy of honor.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

750 lokasvāmī लोकस्वामी Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Lord of the worlds.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

751 trilokadhṛk त्रिलोकधृक् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Supporter of the three worlds.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

752 sumedhā सुमेधा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of excellent intelligence.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

753 medhajaḥ मेधजः Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: Born of sacred intelligence or sacrifice.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an Upasana-friendly name that pulls outward worship back toward the one inner receiver of all sacred action.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by remembering that the deepest oblation is ego-centeredness itself.

Story: The Rajasuya of Yudhishthira culminates in Krishna receiving first honors, showing that all offerings find their center in him.

754 dhanyaḥ धन्यः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Blessed and blessable.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

755 satyamedhā सत्यमेधा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of true intelligence.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

756 dharādharaḥ धराधरः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Supporter of the earth.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a way of reading the elements as nama-rupa dependent on one underlying reality.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by turning sky, light, wind, and water into meditation prompts instead of background scenery.

Story: The serpent Ananta becomes Vishnu's couch, revealing even immense natural force as restful service in the divine presence.

757 tejovṛṣaḥ तेजोवृषः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Showering brilliance.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

758 dyutidharaḥ द्युतिधरः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Bearer of splendor.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

759 sarvaśastrabhṛtāṁ varaḥ सर्वशस्त्रभृतां वरः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Best among all bearers of weapons.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

760 pragrahaḥ प्रग्रहः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The gatherer, restrainer.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

761 nigrahaḥ निग्रहः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The controller, corrector.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

762 vyagraḥ व्यग्रः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Intensely alert and active.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

763 naikaśṛṅgaḥ नैकशृङ्गः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of many horns or peaks.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

764 gadāgrajaḥ गदाग्रजः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Elder to Gada, or primordial before force.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

765 caturmūrtiḥ चतुर्मूर्तिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Four-formed.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

766 caturbāhuḥ चतुर्बाहुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Four-armed.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

767 caturvyūhaḥ चतुर्व्यूहः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of the four emanations.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

768 caturgatiḥ चतुर्गतिः Freedom, destination, and moksha

Meaning: Goal in four paths.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that the Lord praised here is also the final truth in whose knowledge bondage ends.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using sorrow itself as a doorway to discern the difference between passing states and the real.

Story: Prahlada's freedom is interior even before external rescue arrives; this name belongs to the Lord who liberates fear at its root.

769 caturātmā चतुरात्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Fourfold in being.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

770 caturbhāvaḥ चतुर्भावः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Source of the four aims or inner states.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

771 caturvedavit चतुर्वेदविद् Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Knower of the four Vedas.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a contemplative bridge from sacred word to direct recognition of the self-luminous reality.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by letting the name turn curiosity into discrimination and discrimination into stillness.

Story: Sanatkumara and the sages seek the supreme truth and find it centered in Narayana; the name suits the Lord who is both teacher and taught-of.

772 ekapāt एकपात् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The one-footed or singular support.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

773 samāvartaḥ समावर्तः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He who turns the cycle completely.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

774 nivṛttātmā निवृत्तात्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Withdrawn from worldly bondage.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

775 durjayaḥ दुर्जयः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Hard to conquer.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

776 duratikramaḥ दुरतिक्रमः Rama-centered names

Meaning: Impossible to transgress.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an upasana-rich name: form is honored as a valid doorway through which the mind approaches the formless truth.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by meditating on the form lovingly while remembering that the form is a doorway, not a boundary.

Story: Rama's protection of the sages in Dandaka reveals divine kingship expressed through service.

777 durlabhaḥ दुर्लभः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Hard to attain.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

778 durgamaḥ दुर्गमः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Hard to approach.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

779 durgaḥ दुर्गः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The difficult fortress, strong refuge.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

780 durāvāsaḥ दुरावासः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Hard to dwell with or comprehend fully.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

781 durārihā दुरारिहा Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Destroyer of formidable foes.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a support for devotion to the Lord who removes obstacles to knowledge by preserving righteousness.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by seeing the removal of confusion as one of the Lord's most important forms of protection.

Story: Draupadi's helpless cry draws Krishna's intervention in the assembly; the name suits the Lord who protects dignity when human power fails.

782 śubhāṅgaḥ शुभाङ्गः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Of auspicious limbs.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a name in which Sri becomes a contemplative symbol of harmony, grace, and all-sided completeness.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by redefining prosperity as inward clarity plus wise stewardship of what has been given.

Story: Rukmini's heart turns unwaveringly toward Krishna before their marriage; this name reflects divine beauty that draws love by its own worth.

783 lokasāraṅgaḥ लोकसारङ्गः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Essence of the worlds, seeker of their heart.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

784 sutantuḥ सुतन्तुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Fine thread of the universe.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

785 tantuvardhanaḥ तन्तुवर्धनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Expander of the cosmic thread.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

786 indrakarmā इन्द्रकर्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Performer of mighty deeds.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

787 mahākarmā महाकर्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Doer of great acts.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

788 kṛtakarmā कृतकर्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He who has accomplished all acts.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

789 kṛtāgamaḥ कृतागमः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Source of the sacred traditions.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

790 udbhavaḥ उद्भवः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Source and arising.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

791 sundaraḥ सुन्दरः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Beautiful.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a contemplative hint that what seems manifold in nature rests in one unfragmented truth.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by allowing elemental experience to point beyond itself toward the one reality it depends on.

Story: Garuda's service shows sky, speed, and bird-power offered wholly to the Lord; this name resonates with that elevation.

792 sunandaḥ सुनन्दः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Great joy-giver.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a way of reading the elements as nama-rupa dependent on one underlying reality.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by turning sky, light, wind, and water into meditation prompts instead of background scenery.

Story: The moonlike cooling of Krishna's presence in Vraja often follows scenes of heat and turmoil; such names fit the soothing aspect of divinity in nature.

793 ratnanābhaḥ रत्ननाभः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Jewel-naveled.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

794 sulocanaḥ सुलोचनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Beautiful-eyed.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

795 arkaḥ अर्कः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Sun-like radiance.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that cosmic texture is not independent substance but appearance upheld in consciousness.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by observing change in the world while remembering the changeless that makes change knowable.

Story: In the forest episodes of the Ramayana, trees, rivers, and mountains become witnesses to Rama's dharma; this name turns the landscape into theology.

796 vājasanaḥ वाजसनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Giver of strength and nourishment.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

797 śṛṅgī शृङ्गी Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Horned one, or one of exalted summit.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

798 jayantaḥ जयन्तः Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Ever victorious.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a support for devotion to the Lord who removes obstacles to knowledge by preserving righteousness.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by seeing the removal of confusion as one of the Lord's most important forms of protection.

Story: Gajendra's appeal from the lake brings Vishnu immediately on Garuda; this epithet lives in that swift rescue of the desperate.

799 sarvavijjayī सर्वविज्जयी Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Conqueror of all through complete knowledge.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a contemplative bridge from sacred word to direct recognition of the self-luminous reality.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by letting the name turn curiosity into discrimination and discrimination into stillness.

Story: In the Gajendra episode, the elephant's past-life memory of prayer returns at the crucial moment; this name reflects divine knowledge awakening dormant wisdom.

800 suvarṇabinduḥ सुवर्णबिन्दुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Golden point of concentrated splendor.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

Names 801-900

801 akṣobhyaḥ अक्षोभ्यः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Unshakable.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

802 sarvavāgīśvareśvaraḥ सर्ववागीश्वरेश्वरः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Lord of all lords of speech.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a revelation of Vishnu as witness-consciousness and as the light by which scripture and experience become intelligible.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by joining study, self-inquiry, and devotion instead of separating them into different compartments.

Story: Krishna instructs Arjuna on the battlefield, turning despair into discernment; this name belongs to the Lord who teaches inside crisis.

803 mahāhradaḥ महाह्रदः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Great unfathomable lake.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

804 mahāgartaḥ महागर्तः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Great abyss.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

805 mahābhūtaḥ महाभूतः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Great elemental being.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

806 mahānidhiḥ महानिधिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Great treasure.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

807 kumudaḥ कुमुदः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Delighter of the earth.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a contemplative hint that what seems manifold in nature rests in one unfragmented truth.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by allowing elemental experience to point beyond itself toward the one reality it depends on.

Story: Varaha lifting the earth from cosmic waters makes elemental imagery literal; this name suits Vishnu as guardian of the world-body.

808 kundaraḥ कुन्दरः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Breaker of obstacles; cleaver of earth or evil.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a contemplative hint that what seems manifold in nature rests in one unfragmented truth.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by allowing elemental experience to point beyond itself toward the one reality it depends on.

Story: Kurma supports Mount Mandara during the churning, turning mountain and ocean alike into instruments of divine purpose.

809 kundaḥ कुन्दः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Pure like the jasmine flower.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

810 parjanyaḥ पर्जन्यः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Rain-bearing and life-giving.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

811 pāvanaḥ पावनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Purifier.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

812 anilaḥ अनिलः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Wind, life-breath.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a way of reading the elements as nama-rupa dependent on one underlying reality.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by turning sky, light, wind, and water into meditation prompts instead of background scenery.

Story: Matsya guides the ark through the deluge, showing the Lord present in waters both destructive and protective.

813 amṛtāśaḥ अमृताशः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He whose hope or food is immortality.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

814 amṛtavapuḥ अमृतवपुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of immortal form.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

815 sarvajñaḥ सर्वज्ञः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Omniscient.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

816 sarvatomukhaḥ सर्वतोमुखः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Facing everywhere.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

817 sulabhaḥ सुलभः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Easily accessible to devotion.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

818 suvrataḥ सुव्रतः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of noble vows.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

819 siddhaḥ सिद्धः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Perfect.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

820 śatrujit शत्रुजित् Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Conqueror of enemies.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a saguna-Ishvara name through which grace, order, and dharmic correction become meditatively approachable.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by trusting order without becoming passive, and doing your dharma while leaving total control to the Lord.

Story: Rama defends the sages of the forest and keeps his father's word despite hardship; this name honors steadfast protective dharma.

821 śatrutāpanaḥ शत्रुतापनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Scorcher of enemies.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

822 nyagrodhaḥ न्यग्रोधः Compassion, refuge, and tenderness

Meaning: The banyan; sheltering vastness.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that in Advaita too, devotion and divine responsiveness are not bypassed but deepened.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name when you feel impure or unready; it reminds the heart that purification is possible.

Story: Sudama's simple offering of flattened rice is received by Krishna with overwhelming tenderness; this name fits the Lord who values love over polish.

823 udumbaraḥ उदुम्बरः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The sacred fig, source of nourishment.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

824 aśvatthaḥ अश्वत्थः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: The peepal tree; cosmic tree.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that cosmic texture is not independent substance but appearance upheld in consciousness.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by observing change in the world while remembering the changeless that makes change knowable.

Story: Krishna dances upon Kaliya in the Yamuna, purifying poisoned waters; this name aligns with divine mastery within nature.

825 cāṇūrāndhra-niṣūdanaḥ चाणूरान्ध्रनिषूदनः Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Destroyer of Cāṇūra and allied foes.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a support for devotion to the Lord who removes obstacles to knowledge by preserving righteousness.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by seeing the removal of confusion as one of the Lord's most important forms of protection.

Story: Vishnu destroys Madhu and Kaitabha when cosmic order is threatened; the story shows protection at the scale of creation itself.

826 sahasrārciḥ सहस्रार्चिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Thousand-rayed.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

827 saptajihvaḥ सप्तजिह्वः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Seven-tongued like sacrificial fire.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a contemplative hint that what seems manifold in nature rests in one unfragmented truth.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by allowing elemental experience to point beyond itself toward the one reality it depends on.

Story: Govardhana itself becomes a sign of worship when Krishna turns the community toward the mountain, cows, and rain that sustain life.

828 saptaidhāḥ सप्तैधाः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Kindled in seven forms.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

829 saptavāhanaḥ सप्तवाहनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He whose light rides in seven ways.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

830 amūrtiḥ अमूर्तिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Formless.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

831 anaghaḥ अनघः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Sinless.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

832 acintyaḥ अचिन्त्यः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Inconceivable.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

833 bhayakṛt भयकृत् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Giver of fear to the wicked.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

834 bhayanāśanaḥ भयनाशनः Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Destroyer of fear for devotees.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a support for devotion to the Lord who removes obstacles to knowledge by preserving righteousness.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by seeing the removal of confusion as one of the Lord's most important forms of protection.

Story: Krishna breaks Kamsa's reign after years of hidden preparation; this name reflects a protection that can be patient before it becomes dramatic.

835 aṇuḥ अणुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Subtle like the atom.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

836 bṛhat बृहत् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Vast and expansive.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

837 kṛśaḥ कृशः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Fine and subtle.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

838 sthūlaḥ स्थूलः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Gross and manifest.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

839 guṇabhṛt गुणभृत् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Supporter of the qualities of nature.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

840 nirguṇaḥ निर्गुणः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Beyond all qualities.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

841 mahān महान् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The great one.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

842 adhṛtaḥ अधृतः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Unsupported by anything else.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

843 svadhṛtaḥ स्वधृतः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Self-supported.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

844 svāsyaḥ स्वास्यः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Of beautiful face or noble speech.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a contemplative bridge from sacred word to direct recognition of the self-luminous reality.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by letting the name turn curiosity into discrimination and discrimination into stillness.

Story: As Hayagriva, Vishnu restores the Vedas after they are stolen; the story suits names tied to knowledge and sacred speech.

845 prāgvaṁśaḥ प्राग्वंशः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Ancient lineage, primordial source.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

846 vaṁśavardhanaḥ वंशवर्धनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: One who expands the lineage of dharma.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

847 bhārabhṛt भारभृत् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Bearer of burdens.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

848 kathitaḥ कथितः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Well proclaimed in scripture.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

849 yogī योगी Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The yogin.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

850 yogīśaḥ योगीशः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Lord of yogins.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

851 sarvakāmadaḥ सर्वकामदः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Giver of all worthy aims.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

852 āśramaḥ आश्रमः Rama-centered names

Meaning: Resting place and stage of discipline.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a form-centered praise that steadies devotion while quietly training the mind toward the limitless reality behind form.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using personal devotion as the emotional purification that prepares the mind for knowledge.

Story: The defeat of Ravana manifests justice that answers long-standing misuse of power.

853 śramaṇaḥ श्रमणः Yoga, inwardness, and steadiness

Meaning: He who labors for the good, ascetic one.
