Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a name especially useful for shifting from conceptual study into stillness.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by letting silence become a way of listening to the presence already nearer than thought.

Story: Prahlada remembers Hari continuously even under torture; this name reflects yoga as unbroken recollection.

854 kṣāmaḥ क्षामः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Lean through austerity.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

855 suparṇaḥ सुपर्णः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Beautiful-winged, Garuda-like.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

856 vāyuvāhanaḥ वायुवाहनः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Driver of the wind.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a way of reading the elements as nama-rupa dependent on one underlying reality.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by turning sky, light, wind, and water into meditation prompts instead of background scenery.

Story: The descent of the Ganga through divine mediation reflects a cosmos where sacred waters carry grace into the world.

857 dhanurdharaḥ धनुर्धरः Weapons, emblems, and corrective power

Meaning: Wielder of the bow.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a symbolic praise of the Lord's power to cut avidya, protect dharma, and remove inner obstruction.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by letting discrimination cut confusion before it grows into habit.

Story: Kaumodaki, the mace, stands for solid strength in defense of order; such weapon names remind us that dharma needs force as well as ideals.

858 dhanurvedaḥ धनुर्वेदः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Knower of archery and martial wisdom.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a name that links devotion to viveka, because the Lord is both the revealer of truth and the ground of knowing.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by asking not only what is happening, but who is the knower in whose light the happening is known.

Story: Kapila teaches Devahuti the way of liberation and right seeing; this name resonates with Vishnu as clarifying intelligence.

859 daṇḍaḥ दण्डः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The rod of discipline.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

860 damayitā दमयिता Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The tamer.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

861 damaḥ दमः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Self-control.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

862 aparājitaḥ अपराजितः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Unconquered.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

863 sarvasahaḥ सर्वसहः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: All-enduring.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

864 niyantā नियन्ता Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The controller.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

865 aniyamaḥ अनियमः Time, rhythm, and ordinance

Meaning: Beyond all limiting rules.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a name that places time, sequence, and change inside the governance of timeless reality.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by accepting timing, patience, and ripening as part of a sacred process rather than an insult to your plans.

Story: The return of the monsoon after Govardhana's lesson reminds Indra that power too has a rightful rhythm under higher law.

866 ayamaḥ अयमः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Not confined by restraint or death.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

867 sattvavān सत्त्ववान् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Rich in purity and courage.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

868 sāttvikaḥ सात्त्विकः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Pure in essence.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

869 satyaḥ सत्यः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Truth itself.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a name that links devotion to viveka, because the Lord is both the revealer of truth and the ground of knowing.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by asking not only what is happening, but who is the knower in whose light the happening is known.

Story: Uddhava receives Krishna's final teachings at the end of the Yadava era; this epithet shines in wisdom given before departure.

870 satyadharmaparāyaṇaḥ सत्यधर्मपरायणः Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Wholly devoted to truth and dharma.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a contemplative bridge from sacred word to direct recognition of the self-luminous reality.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by letting the name turn curiosity into discrimination and discrimination into stillness.

Story: The child Dhruva receives mantra and then direct vision, showing that true knowledge matures from remembrance into realization.

871 abhiprāyaḥ अभिप्रायः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Inner intent, divine purpose.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

872 priyārhaḥ प्रियार्हः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Worthy of love.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

873 arhaḥ अर्हः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Worthy of worship.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

874 priyakṛt प्रियकृत् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Doer of what is dear and beneficial.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

875 prītivardhanaḥ प्रीतिवर्धनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Increaser of love.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

876 vihāyasagatiḥ विहायसगतिः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Moving through the sky.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that cosmic texture is not independent substance but appearance upheld in consciousness.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by observing change in the world while remembering the changeless that makes change knowable.

Story: The serpent Ananta becomes Vishnu's couch, revealing even immense natural force as restful service in the divine presence.

877 jyotiḥ ज्योतिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Light itself.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

878 suruciḥ सुरुचिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of lovely brilliance.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

879 hutabhuk हुतभुक् Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: Consumer of the oblation.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an Upasana-friendly name that pulls outward worship back toward the one inner receiver of all sacred action.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by remembering that the deepest oblation is ego-centeredness itself.

Story: Daksha's disrupted sacrifice is restored only when the deeper principle of divine order is honored; this name suits Vishnu as the true sanctifier of ritual.

880 vibhuḥ विभुः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The all-pervading mighty one.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

881 raviḥ रविः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: The sun.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a contemplative hint that what seems manifold in nature rests in one unfragmented truth.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by allowing elemental experience to point beyond itself toward the one reality it depends on.

Story: Garuda's service shows sky, speed, and bird-power offered wholly to the Lord; this name resonates with that elevation.

882 virocanaḥ विरोचनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The shining one.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

883 sūryaḥ सूर्यः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Solar radiance.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

884 savitā सविता Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The impeller, the vivifier.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

885 ravilocanaḥ रविलोचनः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Whose eye is the sun.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that cosmic texture is not independent substance but appearance upheld in consciousness.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by observing change in the world while remembering the changeless that makes change knowable.

Story: The moonlike cooling of Krishna's presence in Vraja often follows scenes of heat and turmoil; such names fit the soothing aspect of divinity in nature.

886 anantaḥ अनन्तः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Endless.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

887 hutabhuk हुतभुक् Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: Receiver of sacred offering.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that ritual is fulfilled when it matures into inner offering and knowledge of the divine center.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by treating daily action as worship that should purify the mind, not merely accumulate results.

Story: When Krishna teaches that offerings made with devotion are accepted even if simple, this epithet becomes inward and universal.

888 bhoktā भोक्ता Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Enjoyer.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

889 sukhadaḥ सुखदः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Giver of happiness.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

890 naikajaḥ नैकजः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Born in many ways, manifest in many forms.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

891 agrajaḥ अग्रजः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The first-born, the one before all.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

892 anirviṇṇaḥ अनिर्विण्णः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Never despondent.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

893 sadāmarṣī सदामर्षी Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Ever patient.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

894 lokādhiṣṭhānam लोकाधिष्ठानम् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Foundation of the worlds.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

895 adbhutaḥ अद्भुतः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Wonderful and wondrous.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

896 sanāt सनात् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Ancient, eternal.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

897 sanātanatamaḥ सनातनतमः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The most eternal.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

898 kapilaḥ कपिलः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Tawny-hued; also sage Kapila.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

899 kapiḥ कपिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The radiant one, or the boar/monkey-linked form.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

900 avyayaḥ अव्ययः Freedom, destination, and moksha

Meaning: Imperishable.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a direct pointer toward moksha as knowledge of reality rather than as a mere improved condition inside samsara.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by asking what you still take to be absolutely necessary even though it cannot survive change.

Story: Uddhava is guided beyond grief by Krishna's final teachings; the name suits liberation through understanding and surrender.

Names 901-1000

901 svastidaḥ स्वस्तिदः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Giver of well-being.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

902 svastikṛt स्वस्तिकृत् Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Maker of auspiciousness.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a praise of auspicious fullness in Ishvara: beauty and order serving as supports for contemplation.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by keeping outer order, gratitude, and cleanliness so the mind becomes fit for sacred attention.

Story: When Sudama visits Krishna in poverty and returns to quiet abundance, the story shows auspicious grace arriving without humiliation.

903 svasti स्वस्ति Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Auspiciousness itself.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that sacred radiance is not opposed to truth but can refine the mind toward it.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by seeing beauty as a support for recollection rather than as a distraction to be resented.

Story: The Lord seated with Sri on his chest symbolizes a kingship inseparable from compassion and harmony; this name points to that inseparable splendor.

904 svastibhuk स्वस्तिभुक् Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Enjoyer of auspicious offerings.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that sacred radiance is not opposed to truth but can refine the mind toward it.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by seeing beauty as a support for recollection rather than as a distraction to be resented.

Story: Tulasi's devotion becomes eternally joined to Vishnu's worship, showing that auspiciousness includes purity of offering and love.

905 svastidakṣiṇaḥ स्वस्तिदक्षिणः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Giver of auspicious rewards.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that sacred radiance is not opposed to truth but can refine the mind toward it.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by seeing beauty as a support for recollection rather than as a distraction to be resented.

Story: The Samudra Manthana produces many treasures, yet Lakshmi's choosing of Vishnu reveals where true prosperity ultimately rests.

906 araudraḥ अरौद्रः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Not cruel, free from wrath toward devotees.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

907 kuṇḍalī कुंडली Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Wearer of earrings; coiled power.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

908 cakrī चक्री Weapons, emblems, and corrective power

Meaning: Bearer of the discus.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that even martial imagery can serve Advaita by depicting the destruction of misapprehension.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by treating discipline as grace in active form.

Story: Nandaka, the sword of wisdom, is often read as cutting ignorance; this name suits divine intervention that clarifies as it conquers.

909 vikramī विक्रमी Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Heroic in stride.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

910 ūrjitaśāsanaḥ ऊर्जितशासनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of powerful command.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

911 śabdātigaḥ शब्दातिगः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Beyond sound and verbal definition.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

912 śabdasahaḥ शब्दसहः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Patient with sound; supporter of sacred word.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

913 śiśiraḥ शिशिरः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Cooling and soothing.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

914 śarvarīkaraḥ शर्वरीकरः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Maker of the night or rest-giver.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

915 akrūraḥ अक्रूरः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Not cruel; also linked with Akrura.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

916 peśalaḥ पेशलः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Graceful and refined.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

917 dakṣaḥ दक्षः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Skillful.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

918 dakṣiṇaḥ दक्षिणः Compassion, refuge, and tenderness

Meaning: Gentle, skillful, and giving.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a way of praising the Lord whose grace purifies the mind and makes brahma-jnana possible.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by accepting grace as a real part of sadhana and allowing humility to soften self-judgment.

Story: Sabari's patient devotion is fulfilled when Rama accepts her offering without contempt; the name reflects divine gentleness toward sincerity.

919 kṣamiṇāṁ varaḥ क्षमिणां वरः Compassion, refuge, and tenderness

Meaning: Best among the forgiving.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a way of praising the Lord whose grace purifies the mind and makes brahma-jnana possible.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by accepting grace as a real part of sadhana and allowing humility to soften self-judgment.

Story: Ajamila is rescued by the power of Narayana's name even after a fallen life; this epithet shows mercy reaching where merit cannot.

920 vidvattamaḥ विद्वत्तमः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Greatest among the wise.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

921 vītabhayaḥ वीतभयः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Free from fear and remover of fear.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

922 puṇyaśravaṇakīrtanaḥ पुण्यश्रवणकीर्तनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Whose hearing and chanting are meritorious.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

923 uttāraṇaḥ उत्तारणः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He who lifts across.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

924 duṣkṛtihā दुष्कृतिहा Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Destroyer of misdeeds.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a support for devotion to the Lord who removes obstacles to knowledge by preserving righteousness.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by seeing the removal of confusion as one of the Lord's most important forms of protection.

Story: When Banasura's arrogance overwhelms his own good fortune, Krishna defeats him without needless cruelty; this name shows protective justice with restraint.

925 puṇyaḥ पुण्यः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Holy one.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

926 duḥsvapnanāśanaḥ दुःस्वप्ननाशनः Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Destroyer of bad dreams.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a support for devotion to the Lord who removes obstacles to knowledge by preserving righteousness.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by seeing the removal of confusion as one of the Lord's most important forms of protection.

Story: In saving the elephant king, Vishnu accepts no delay because the cry is sincere; that immediacy illustrates this name.

927 vīrahā वीरहा Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Destroyer of mighty foes.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a saguna-Ishvara name through which grace, order, and dharmic correction become meditatively approachable.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by trusting order without becoming passive, and doing your dharma while leaving total control to the Lord.

Story: The Sudarshana protects Ambarisha from Durvasa's fury, proving this name through preservation of the humble devotee.

928 rakṣaṇaḥ रक्षणः Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Protector.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a saguna-Ishvara name through which grace, order, and dharmic correction become meditatively approachable.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by trusting order without becoming passive, and doing your dharma while leaving total control to the Lord.

Story: Krishna safeguards Parikshit in the womb from Ashvatthama's weapon; the name fits that unseen guardianship of future dharma.

929 santaḥ सन्तः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The peaceful, saintly one.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

930 jīvanaḥ जीवनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Life-giver.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

931 paryavasthitaḥ पर्यवस्थितः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Established everywhere around all.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

932 anantarūpaḥ अनन्तरूपः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of endless forms.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

933 anantaśrīḥ अनन्तश्रीः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Of infinite splendor.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a name in which Sri becomes a contemplative symbol of harmony, grace, and all-sided completeness.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by redefining prosperity as inward clarity plus wise stewardship of what has been given.

Story: In the story of Andal's longing for Ranganatha, divine beauty becomes an object of total surrender; this name resonates with that magnetic grace.

934 jitamanyuḥ जितमन्युः Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Conqueror of wrath.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a support for devotion to the Lord who removes obstacles to knowledge by preserving righteousness.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by seeing the removal of confusion as one of the Lord's most important forms of protection.

Story: As Mohini, Vishnu protects the devas by recovering the amrita from a chaotic scramble; this name reflects strategic preservation.

935 bhayāpahaḥ भयापहः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Remover of fear.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

936 caturaśraḥ चतुरश्रः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Perfectly balanced on every side.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

937 gabhīrātmā गभीरात्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Deep in self.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

938 vidiśaḥ विदिशः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Giver of direction.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

939 vyādiśaḥ व्यादिशः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Ordainer in all directions.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

940 diśaḥ दिशः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The directions themselves.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

941 anādiḥ अनादिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Beginningless.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

942 bhūr bhuvaḥ भूर् भुवः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: The earth and the mid-region.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a contemplative hint that what seems manifold in nature rests in one unfragmented truth.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by allowing elemental experience to point beyond itself toward the one reality it depends on.

Story: In the forest episodes of the Ramayana, trees, rivers, and mountains become witnesses to Rama's dharma; this name turns the landscape into theology.

943 lakṣmīḥ लक्ष्मीः Sri, beauty, and auspicious abundance

Meaning: Auspicious abundance itself.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a name in which Sri becomes a contemplative symbol of harmony, grace, and all-sided completeness.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by redefining prosperity as inward clarity plus wise stewardship of what has been given.

Story: At the emergence of Lakshmi from the milk ocean, she chooses Vishnu as her eternal abode; this name naturally carries the glow of that union.

944 suvīraḥ सुवीरः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Of noble heroism.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

945 rucirāṅgadaḥ रुचिराङ्गदः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Adorned with shining armlets.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

946 jananaḥ जननः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The generator.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

947 janajanmādiḥ जनजन्मादिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Source of the birth of beings.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

948 bhīmaḥ भीमः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Terrible to evil.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

949 bhīmaparākramaḥ भीमपराक्रमः Rama-centered names

Meaning: Of formidable valor.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that avatara names are not metaphysical limitations of Brahman but compassionate manifestations for seekers.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by allowing the story to hold the heart steady until deeper understanding becomes possible.

Story: Sabari's darshana shows Rama's tenderness toward humble devotion.

950 ādhāranilayaḥ आधारनिलयः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Foundation of all support.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

951 dhātā धाता Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The sustainer.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

952 puṣpahāsaḥ पुष्पहासः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: He who blooms like a flower.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

953 prajāgaraḥ प्रजागरः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Ever awake.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

954 ūrdhvagaḥ ऊर्ध्वगः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Ever-rising, transcendently above.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

955 satpathācāraḥ सत्पथाचारः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Walker in the true path.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

956 prāṇadaḥ प्राणदः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Giver of life.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

957 praṇavaḥ प्रणवः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The sacred Om.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

958 paṇaḥ पणः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The cosmic exchange or stake.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

959 pramāṇam प्रमाणम् Time, rhythm, and ordinance

Meaning: The measure and authority.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that the Lord presides over temporal process while remaining untouched by its mutations.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name when restless about results; it helps distinguish eternal value from temporary sequence.

Story: Krishna's promise that dharma is restored age after age frames this name in the long continuity of divine intervention.

960 prāṇanilayaḥ प्राणनिलयः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Abode of life-breath.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

961 prāṇabhṛt प्राणभृत् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Supporter of life.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

962 prāṇajīvanaḥ प्राणजीवनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Life of life itself.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: When mother Yashoda asked Krishna to open his mouth, she saw worlds within him; this name fits the Lord whose apparent smallness can suddenly reveal totality.

963 tattvam तत्त्वम् Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Reality, truth.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a revelation of Vishnu as witness-consciousness and as the light by which scripture and experience become intelligible.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by joining study, self-inquiry, and devotion instead of separating them into different compartments.

Story: When Brahma is humbled by Krishna's play in Vraja, he gains clearer understanding of the Lord's inconceivable nature; this name fits that awakening.

964 tattvavit तत्त्ववित् Knowledge, Veda, and revelation

Meaning: Knower of reality.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a revelation of Vishnu as witness-consciousness and as the light by which scripture and experience become intelligible.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by joining study, self-inquiry, and devotion instead of separating them into different compartments.

Story: Narada repeatedly carries divine insight into confused situations, and his guidance ultimately points back to Vishnu; this name aligns with such illuminating grace.

965 ekātmā एकात्मा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The one self in all.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Gita's Vishvarupa revelation, Arjuna beholds all beings and all time in Krishna; this name shines where Vishnu appears as the cosmic whole.

966 janmamṛtyujarātigaḥ जन्ममृत्युजरातिगः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Beyond birth, death, and old age.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: During pralaya, Markandeya sees the infant Narayana on the banyan leaf holding the universe in serene play; that vision matches this boundless epithet.

967 bhūrbhuvaḥsvastaruḥ भूर्भुवःस्वस्तरुः Compassion, refuge, and tenderness

Meaning: The sheltering tree of all the worlds.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that in Advaita too, devotion and divine responsiveness are not bypassed but deepened.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name when you feel impure or unready; it reminds the heart that purification is possible.

Story: Kubja, bent by circumstance, is straightened by Krishna's gracious touch; the name fits a Lord who restores dignity as well as form.

968 tāraḥ तारः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The deliverer.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: From the lotus springing out of Vishnu's navel, Brahma begins creation; this name recalls the Lord as the hidden ground of all worlds.

969 savitā सविता Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: The vivifier.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Vamana's three strides cover earth, heaven, and the regions between; this name becomes visible in that act of all-pervasion.

970 prapitāmahaḥ प्रपितामहः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Ancestor even of the grandsire.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Prahlada insists that Hari is present everywhere, even in a pillar; this name is affirmed by that fearless vision of omnipresence.

971 yajñaḥ यज्ञः Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: Sacrifice itself.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an Upasana-friendly name that pulls outward worship back toward the one inner receiver of all sacred action.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by remembering that the deepest oblation is ego-centeredness itself.

Story: Bali's gift to Vamana turns a ritual act into total surrender; this name fits the Lord who receives both offering and offerer.

972 yajñapatiḥ यज्ञपतिः Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: Lord of sacrifice.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a way of seeing sacrifice, offerer, offering, and fruit as resting in one Brahman through Ishvara.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by offering speech, labor, food, and restraint inwardly to the Lord rather than performing them with vanity.

Story: The sages at Naimisharanya keep sacrificial time alive through remembrance of Narayana; the name belongs to the divine heart of ritual continuity.

973 yajvā यज्वा Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: Performer of sacrifice.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a way of seeing sacrifice, offerer, offering, and fruit as resting in one Brahman through Ishvara.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by offering speech, labor, food, and restraint inwardly to the Lord rather than performing them with vanity.

Story: In Ambarisha's observance of Ekadashi and Dvadashi, discipline itself becomes worship; this name suits Vishnu as the fulfiller of vowed offering.

974 yajñāṅgaḥ यज्ञाङ्गः Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: Limb of sacrifice.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a way of seeing sacrifice, offerer, offering, and fruit as resting in one Brahman through Ishvara.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by offering speech, labor, food, and restraint inwardly to the Lord rather than performing them with vanity.

Story: In the churning of the ocean, gods and asuras labor for nectar, but the whole enterprise reaches order only through Vishnu's oversight; this name shows the Lord as the hidden receiver of sacrifice.

975 yajñavāhanaḥ यज्ञवाहनः Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: Carrier of sacrifice.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that ritual is fulfilled when it matures into inner offering and knowledge of the divine center.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by treating daily action as worship that should purify the mind, not merely accumulate results.

Story: As Yajna in primordial times, Vishnu upholds cosmic order through sacrificial harmony; the name fits the deity who is both rite and fruit.

976 yajñabhṛt यज्ञभृत् Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: Supporter of sacrifice.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that ritual is fulfilled when it matures into inner offering and knowledge of the divine center.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by treating daily action as worship that should purify the mind, not merely accumulate results.

Story: The Rajasuya of Yudhishthira culminates in Krishna receiving first honors, showing that all offerings find their center in him.

977 yajñakṛt यज्ञकृत् Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: Performer and maker of sacrifice.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that ritual is fulfilled when it matures into inner offering and knowledge of the divine center.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by treating daily action as worship that should purify the mind, not merely accumulate results.

Story: Daksha's disrupted sacrifice is restored only when the deeper principle of divine order is honored; this name suits Vishnu as the true sanctifier of ritual.

978 yajñī यज्ञी Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: The one who is worshipped through sacrifice.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as an Upasana-friendly name that pulls outward worship back toward the one inner receiver of all sacred action.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by remembering that the deepest oblation is ego-centeredness itself.

Story: When Krishna teaches that offerings made with devotion are accepted even if simple, this epithet becomes inward and universal.

979 yajñabhuk यज्ञभुक् Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: Enjoyer of sacrifice.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an Upasana-friendly name that pulls outward worship back toward the one inner receiver of all sacred action.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by remembering that the deepest oblation is ego-centeredness itself.

Story: Bali's gift to Vamana turns a ritual act into total surrender; this name fits the Lord who receives both offering and offerer.

980 yajñasādhanaḥ यज्ञसाधनः Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: Means by which sacrifice is accomplished.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes an Upasana-friendly name that pulls outward worship back toward the one inner receiver of all sacred action.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by remembering that the deepest oblation is ego-centeredness itself.

Story: The sages at Naimisharanya keep sacrificial time alive through remembrance of Narayana; the name belongs to the divine heart of ritual continuity.

981 yajñāntakṛt यज्ञान्तकृत् Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: He who brings sacrifice to completion.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a way of seeing sacrifice, offerer, offering, and fruit as resting in one Brahman through Ishvara.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by offering speech, labor, food, and restraint inwardly to the Lord rather than performing them with vanity.

Story: In Ambarisha's observance of Ekadashi and Dvadashi, discipline itself becomes worship; this name suits Vishnu as the fulfiller of vowed offering.

982 yajñaguhyam यज्ञगुह्यम् Yajna, worship, and sacred order

Meaning: The secret of sacrifice.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a way of seeing sacrifice, offerer, offering, and fruit as resting in one Brahman through Ishvara.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by offering speech, labor, food, and restraint inwardly to the Lord rather than performing them with vanity.

Story: In the churning of the ocean, gods and asuras labor for nectar, but the whole enterprise reaches order only through Vishnu's oversight; this name shows the Lord as the hidden receiver of sacrifice.

983 annam अन्नम् Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Food itself.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: In the Bhagavata, Krishna expands himself for every gopa and calf during Brahma's bewilderment; this name suits the Lord who is one yet all.

984 annādaḥ अन्नादः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Eater and giver of food.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Narayana resting on the cosmic ocean before manifest creation embodies this name as quiet totality before differentiation.

985 ātmayoniḥ आत्मयोनिः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Self-born source.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: When Akrura enters the Yamuna and beholds Vishnu with Ananta, he sees the transcendent form within the ordinary world; that is this name in narrative form.

986 svayaṁjātaḥ स्वयंजातः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Self-manifest.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a reminder that Vishnu is not one object in the universe, but the inner reality because of which the universe appears.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by pausing during ordinary activity and noticing that every experience shines in the same field of awareness.

Story: Dhruva's austerity ends in a vision of the Lord whose presence fills inner and outer space alike; this name belongs to that overwhelming encounter.

987 vaikhānaḥ वैखानः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: One who has dug out or manifested the earth; also linked with Vaikhanasa tradition.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a contemplative hint that what seems manifold in nature rests in one unfragmented truth.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by allowing elemental experience to point beyond itself toward the one reality it depends on.

Story: Varaha lifting the earth from cosmic waters makes elemental imagery literal; this name suits Vishnu as guardian of the world-body.

988 sāmagāyanaḥ सामगायनः Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Singer of the Sama.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as an aid to sarvatmakatva: the recognition that the one reality shines through the entire field of experience.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by using the name to move from scattered involvement toward the intuition of a single underlying reality.

Story: Garuda carries the Lord across the worlds, yet no place contains him; the story reinforces this name's claim that Vishnu transcends location.

989 devakīnandanaḥ देवकीनन्दनः Krishna-centered names

Meaning: Joy of Devaki.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a form-centered praise that steadies devotion while quietly training the mind toward the limitless reality behind form.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by using personal devotion as the emotional purification that prepares the mind for knowledge.

Story: The Kaliya dance reveals mastery over poison without loss of grace; this story fits many active Krishna epithets.

990 sraṣṭā स्रष्टा Cosmic and all-pervading names

Meaning: Creator.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a pointer to Brahman as the substratum of all names, forms, causes, and effects.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by loosening the habit of seeing life as spiritually fragmented and remembering one presence through every perception.

Story: At the close of each kalpa, the worlds fold back into Narayana; this name points to that cosmic re-absorption.

991 kṣitīśaḥ क्षितीशः Nature, elements, and cosmic embodiment

Meaning: Lord of the earth.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a way of reading the elements as nama-rupa dependent on one underlying reality.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by turning sky, light, wind, and water into meditation prompts instead of background scenery.

Story: Kurma supports Mount Mandara during the churning, turning mountain and ocean alike into instruments of divine purpose.

992 pāpanāśanaḥ पापनाशनः Protection, justice, and divine strength

Meaning: Destroyer of sin.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a saguna-Ishvara name through which grace, order, and dharmic correction become meditatively approachable.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by trusting order without becoming passive, and doing your dharma while leaving total control to the Lord.

Story: Narasimha bursts forth to save Prahlada and break tyrannical pride; this name fits the Lord whose protection arrives exactly where hope seems blocked.

993 śaṅkhabhṛt शङ्खभृत् Weapons, emblems, and corrective power

Meaning: Bearer of the conch.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a vivid devotional image in which divine instruments stand for precise correction in the field of ignorance.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by asking the Lord to remove inner enemies with the same decisiveness shown in sacred iconography.

Story: When Vishnu's many arms bear many implements in iconography, the image teaches that the Lord meets different disorders with fitting remedies.

994 nandakī नन्दकी Weapons, emblems, and corrective power

Meaning: Bearer of the Nandaka sword.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a vivid devotional image in which divine instruments stand for precise correction in the field of ignorance.

How to contemplate: Relate to it in practice by asking the Lord to remove inner enemies with the same decisiveness shown in sacred iconography.

Story: Sudarshana pursues Durvasa to defend Ambarisha, showing that divine weaponry protects bhakti rather than egoic ambition.

995 cakrī चक्री Weapons, emblems, and corrective power

Meaning: Bearer of the discus.

Shankara lens: In an Advaita mode of contemplation, this epithet becomes a vivid devotional image in which divine instruments stand for precise correction in the field of ignorance.

How to contemplate: Let it work on the mind by asking the Lord to remove inner enemies with the same decisiveness shown in sacred iconography.

Story: Krishna's discus severs Shishupala's arrogance at the Rajasuya after long patience, making this name one of measured justice.

996 śārṅgadhanvā शार्ङ्गधन्वा Weapons, emblems, and corrective power

Meaning: Wielder of the Sharnga bow.

Shankara lens: In a Shankara-shaped reading, this name works as a reminder that even martial imagery can serve Advaita by depicting the destruction of misapprehension.

How to contemplate: Contemplate it by treating discipline as grace in active form.

Story: In the war against Narakasura, Vishnu's weapons restore balance by removing predatory power; this name fits that corrective force.

997 gadādharaḥ गदाधरः Weapons, emblems, and corrective power

Meaning: Bearer of the mace.

Shankara lens: From the Advaita bhashya lens, this name serves as a reminder that even martial imagery can serve Advaita by depicting the destruction of misapprehension.
